following_context: Supersymmetry has played a central role in shaping our modern understanding of quantum field theory beyond perturbation theory. section_title: Geometric Engineering of Three-Dimensional Supersymmetric Gauge Theories
content: Supersymmetry has played a central role in shaping our modern understanding of quantum field theory beyond perturbation theory. By severely constraining the structure of quantum corrections, supersymmetric theories admit exact results that would otherwise be inaccessible, allowing for the controlled study of strongly coupled dynamics. Among the most striking consequences of these constraints is the existence of nonperturbative dualities: distinct microscopic descriptions that flow to the same infrared fixed point. String theory provides a natural framework in which many such dualities acquire a geometric origin. In particular, extended objects such as D-branes furnish concrete realizations of supersymmetric gauge theories, with their low-energy dynamics captured by worldvolume field theories. Operations that are highly nontrivial from a purely field-theoretic perspective—such as strong weak coupling dualities, symmetry enhancements, or infrared equivalences—often translate into simple geometric manipulations of brane configurations. Three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories offer a particularly rich arena for these ideas. Unlike their four-dimensional counterparts, they exhibit intrinsically nonperturbative operators, such as monopoles, which play a central role in determining the infrared dynamics. At the same time, three-dimensional theories admit a wide class of dualities whose origin is most transparent when viewed through their string-theoretic embeddings. Chief among these is \emph{mirror symmetry} , an infrared duality that exchanges the Higgs and Coulomb branches of $\mathcal N=4$ theories and interchanges perturbative and nonperturbative degrees of freedom. In this chapter, we review the interplay between supersymmetry, brane constructions, and mirror symmetry in three dimensions. We begin with a brief overview of supersymmetry and D-branes and their low-energy worldvolume descriptions, emphasizing the emergence of supersymmetric gauge theories from string theory. We then introduce the Hanany Witten construction, which provides a geometric realization of three-dimensional gauge dynamics and their dualities. Within this framework, mirror symmetry arises naturally as the action of Type IIB $\mathcal S$ -duality, offering a clear geometric interpretation of the exchange between Coulomb and Higgs branch physics. This review serves two purposes. First, it establishes the standard framework for mirror symmetry in $\mathcal N=4$ theories, which will be used extensively throughout this thesis. Second, it motivates the central question addressed in later chapters: how much of this structure survives when supersymmetry is reduced, brane realizations are no longer available, and the classical notions of Higgs and Coulomb branches cease to exist. The techniques and perspectives introduced here will provide the foundation for extending mirror symmetry beyond its traditional setting.

following_context: Supersymmetry provides a powerful theoretical framework in which strongly coupled quantum field theories can be studied with a remarkable degree of analytical control. In three spacetime dimensions, supersymmetric gauge theories exhibit a rich interplay between gauge dynamics, global symmetries, and quantum effects that has led to deep insights into infrared physics, including the existence of nontrivial fixed points and intricate patterns of duality. The purpose of this section is to review the aspects of supersymmetric field theories that will be essential throughout this thesis, with an emphasis on structures that are robust under renormalization group flow and therefore characterize the infrared theory.

content: Supersymmetry provides a powerful theoretical framework in which strongly coupled quantum field theories can be studied with a remarkable degree of analytical control. In three spacetime dimensions, supersymmetric gauge theories exhibit a rich interplay between gauge dynamics, global symmetries, and quantum effects that has led to deep insights into infrared physics, including the existence of nontrivial fixed points and intricate patterns of duality. The purpose of this section is to review the aspects of supersymmetric field theories that will be essential throughout this thesis, with an emphasis on structures that are robust under renormalization group flow and therefore characterize the infrared theory. To that end, we mainly collect and review some key results from . Rather than aiming for a comprehensive introduction to supersymmetry, we focus on the ingredients that play a central role in our study of three-dimensional dynamics. This material will serve both to fix conventions and to establish a common language for discussing infrared dualities, mass deformations, and symmetry realizations in later chapters. In this sense, the section is intended as a conceptual and technical foundation, highlighting those features of supersymmetric gauge theories that persist beyond a specific ultraviolet description and govern the universal infrared behavior.

following_context: The history of supersymmetry is rather peculiar, even when compared to that of most other topics in theoretical physics. Originally proposed in the early 1970s, supersymmetry was progressively developed into a remarkably rich and elegant mathematical framework. It unifies particles of different spins into common symmetry multiplets and has far-reaching implications for both quantum field theory and fundamental physics \parencite Weinberg2015 .

section_title: A Prelude: Supersymmetry in Diverse Dimensions
content: The history of supersymmetry is rather peculiar, even when compared to that of most other topics in theoretical physics. Originally proposed in the early 1970s, supersymmetry was progressively developed into a remarkably rich and elegant mathematical framework. It unifies particles of different spins into common symmetry multiplets and has far-reaching implications for both quantum field theory and fundamental physics \parencite Weinberg2015 . Early attempts to extend the symmetry structure of relativistic quantum field theories were motivated by the idea of combining spacetime symmetries with internal gauge symmetries. In particular, it appeared natural to seek a unified framework incorporating the Poincaré group in four dimensions, $ISO(1,3)$ , together with the gauge group of the Standard Model, $SU(3)_c \times SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ . Concrete proposals along these lines were explored, including attempts to embed these symmetries into a larger group such as $SU(6)$ \parencite Weinberg2015 . However, these ideas were ultimately ruled out by a series of no-go theorems, the most notable of which is the \textbf{Coleman–Mandula theorem} : The most general bosonic symmetry of scattering amplitudes is a direct product of the Poincar \'{e} group and an internal symmetry \parencite Coleman1967

Originally proposed in the early 1970s, supersymmetry was progressively developed into a remarkably rich and elegant mathematical framework. It unifies particles of different spins into common symmetry multiplets and has far-reaching implications for both quantum field theory and fundamental physics \parencite Weinberg2015 . Early attempts to extend the symmetry structure of relativistic quantum field theories were motivated by the idea of combining spacetime symmetries with internal gauge symmetries. In particular, it appeared natural to seek a unified framework incorporating the Poincaré group in four dimensions, $ISO(1,3)$ , together with the gauge group of the Standard Model, $SU(3)_c \times SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y$ . Concrete proposals along these lines were explored, including attempts to embed these symmetries into a larger group such as $SU(6)$ \parencite Weinberg2015 . However, these ideas were ultimately ruled out by a series of no-go theorems, the most notable of which is the \textbf{Coleman–Mandula theorem} : The most general bosonic symmetry of scattering amplitudes is a direct product of the Poincar \'{e} group and an internal symmetry \parencite Coleman1967
following_context: In other words, under the assumptions of the theorem, there exists \emph{no non-trivial way of combining spacetime and internal symmetries} within a purely bosonic framework. However, like all good no-go theorems, the Coleman–Mandula result admits a crucial loophole: it assumes that all symmetry generators are bosonic. Shortly after its formulation, this limitation was exploited with the emergence of supersymmetry.

section_title: A Prelude: Supersymmetry in Diverse Dimensions
content: In other words, under the assumptions of the theorem, there exists \emph{no non-trivial way of combining spacetime and internal symmetries} within a purely bosonic framework. However, like all good no-go theorems, the Coleman–Mandula result admits a crucial loophole: it assumes that all symmetry generators are bosonic. Shortly after its formulation, this limitation was exploited with the emergence of supersymmetry. In 1975, Haag, Łopuszański, and Sohnius extended the theorem to include fermionic symmetry generators, thereby identifying supersymmetry as the most general consistent extension of spacetime symmetries: The most general symmetry of the S-matrix consists of the Poincar \'{e} group, internal symmetries, and additional fermionic generators that form a non-trivial extension of the Poincar \'{e} algebra \parencite Haag:1974qh . The resulting super-Poincar \'{e} algebra is a $\mathbb{Z}_2$ -graded [Footnote: A \emph{graded vector space} is a direct sum over vector spaces $\mathfrak a = \oplus_{i\in\mathbb Z}\mathfrak a_i$ over a field $\mathbb K$ of characteristic zero. The $\mathfrak a_i$ are components of $\mathfrak a$ of degree $i$, and the degree of $a\in \mathfrak a$ is denoted $|a|$. A graded vector space with degree shifted by $n$ is denoted by $\mathfrak a[n]=\oplus_{i\in\mathbb Z}(\mathfrak a[n])_i$ with $(\mathfrak a[n])_i = \mathfrak a_{i+n}$. A \emph{graded Lie Algebra} $\mathfrak{a}$ of degree $n$ is a graded vector space $\mathfrak{a}$ endowed with a graded Lie bracket on $\mathfrak{a}[n]$, that is, a bilinear operation $\{.,.\}:\mathfrak a\otimes\mathfrak a\to \mathfrak a[-n]$ satisfying graded antisymmetry and graded Jacobi relations \cite{CATTANEO2006560} \{a,b\} =-(-1)^(|a|+n)(|b|+n)\{b,a\},\\ \{a,\{b,c\}\} = \{\{a,b\},c\} +(-1)^(|a|+n)(|b|+n)\{a,\{b,c\}\}.] algebra : its even part coincides with the ordinary Poincar \'{e} algebra, while its odd part is generated by spinorial charges. At a physical level, supersymmetry transformations exchange bosonic and fermionic degrees of freedom, mapping particles of integer spin to particles of half-integer spin and vice versa. In this sense, supersymmetry constitutes a natural and minimal extension of the known symmetries of relativistic quantum field theory. Remarkably, supersymmetry is the \emph{only} known symmetry that can relate bosons and fermions in a quantum-consistent way. More generally, it is the only symmetry of the S-matrix capable of connecting states with different spin-statistics. Classical symmetries relating fields of different spin are typically spoiled by quantum effects, unless they are protected by supersymmetry. As a consequence, sufficiently extended supersymmetry is often required for classical geometric and algebraic structures of field theory to survive quantization and retain a precise mathematical meaning . The exact form of the Poincar \'{e} algebra depends on the zoology of spinors in various dimensions and the corresponding space-time signatures. However, the general structure is fairly universal, and we comment on that before focusing on supersymmetry in 3 spacetime dimensions.

content: corresponding to positive and negative chirality. Elements of $\mathbf{S}_\pm$ are called \textit{Weyl spinors} . The properties of spinors depend crucially on the spacetime signature $(p,q)$ . In particular, the spinor space $\mathbf{S}$ may admit a real, complex, or quaternionic structure compatible with the action of $Spin(p,q)$ . This determines the possible reality conditions that can be imposed on spinors. The group $Spin(p,q)$ admits a minimal realization \parencite Postnikov1994 in terms of $2^\alpha \times 2^\alpha$ matrices with entries in $\mathbb{R}$ , $\mathbb{C}$ , or $\mathbb{H}$ , depending on the value of $p-q \ (\mathrm{mod}\ 8)$ , as summarized in Table . \centering \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{14pt} $Spin(p,q)$ groups and their minimal matrix realizations

following_context: We say that in $D$ dimensions with signature $(p,q)$ there exist \textit{Majorana spinors} , respectively \textit{symplectic–Majorana spinors} , if the spinor space $\mathbf{S}$ admits an invariant real, respectively quaternionic, structure under the action of $Spin(p,q)$ . In this case, the corresponding (pseudo)real elements of $\mathbf{S}$ are called Majorana or symplectic–Majorana spinors. If, in addition, the chiral subspaces $\mathbf{S}_\pm$ admit an invariant real or quaternionic structure, the corresponding spinors are called \textit{Majorana–Weyl} or \textit{symplectic–Majorana–Weyl} spinors, respectively.

Hence, in Minkowski signature $(D − 1, 1)$ , the minimal spinor is as in Table . \centering \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{14pt} Minimal spinors in Minkowski space

content: To make manifest the automorphism group of the supersymmetry algebra, it is convenient to write the supercharges in terms of minimal spinors. When the supersymmetry automorphism is a physical symmetry of the theory, we refer to it as the R-symmetry of the theory. In supersymmetric theories, $\mathcal N$ denotes the number of independent supersymmetry generators, counted in units of the minimal spinor appropriate to the spacetime dimension and signature. Equivalently, $\mathcal N$ measures how many copies of the minimal supersymmetry algebra are realized in the theory. Since the dimension and reality properties of spinors depend on the spacetime dimension, a fixed value of $\mathcal N$ corresponds to a different total number of real supercharges in different dimensions. In even dimensions, where chiral spinors exist, supersymmetry may further decompose into independent left- and right-moving sectors, characterized by a pair $(\mathcal N_R,\mathcal N_L)$ . Table summarizes the values of $\mathcal N$ (or $(\mathcal N_R,\mathcal N_L)$ ) that are physically allowed in various spacetime dimensions, subject to spinorial constraints and consistency of the supersymmetry algebra. \centering \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{14pt} Physically allowed $\mathcal N$ s in various dimensions. Note that in two, six, and ten spacetime dimensions, we have independent left/right supersymmetries (see also Table ). Then we have distinct right and left SUSY extension numbers $\mathcal N_R,\,\,\mathcal N_L$ . Bold face numbers correspond to supersymmetric extensions allowed only in the local case (supergravity).

following_context: The results presented in this thesis are rooted in the study of three-dimensional gauge theories. Having established the general structure of the supersymmetry algebra, we now turn to a review of several features that are specific to supersymmetric field theories in three spacetime dimensions. These theories exhibit a number of distinctive properties — both technical and conceptual — that set them apart from their four-dimensional counterparts and make them particularly rich and tractable objects of study [Footnote: As \cite{Cecotti:2015wqa} eloquently remarks, \textit{``3d supersymmetry is to 4d supersymmetry what Diet Coke is to Coke — no sugar, no caffeine, all the flavor.''}] .

content: The results presented in this thesis are rooted in the study of three-dimensional gauge theories. Having established the general structure of the supersymmetry algebra, we now turn to a review of several features that are specific to supersymmetric field theories in three spacetime dimensions. following_context: Representation Theory of the Supersymmetry Algebra. The elementary representations of the $\mathcal{N}=4$ supersymmetry algebra are the vector multiplet and the hypermultiplet. Since the theories studied in this thesis preserve at most $\mathcal{N}=2$ supersymmetry, it is convenient to express these multiplets in terms of $\mathcal{N}=2$ representations.

following_context: where $\mathcal{W}_{\alpha}:=-\tfrac{1}{4}\overline{DD}D_{\alpha}V$ is the gauge field strength superfield, and $D_{\alpha}$ are supersymmetry-covariant derivatives. The Yang Mills coupling has scaling dimension $\tfrac{1}{2}$ , implying that even Abelian theories flow to strong coupling in the infrared. The action of the $\mathcal{N}=4$ R-symmetry is as follows - $SU(2)_C$ acts on the vector multiplet, rotating the three scalars as a triplet, while $SU(2)_H$ acts on hypermultiplets, rotating the complex scalars $Q,\, \tilde{Q}^{\dagger}$ as a doublet.

following_context: A 0-form Topological Symmetry. A distinctive feature of three-dimensional gauge theories is the presence of an additional global $0$ -form symmetry , known as the \emph{topological} or \emph{magnetic} symmetry [Footnote: Also referred to as the NielsenOlesenAbrikosov symmetry \cite{Nielsen:1973cs} by our friendly neighborhood condensed matter theorists.] . following_context: which modifies the equations of motion. In three dimensions, FI parameters may be interpreted as background real masses for the topological $U(1)_T$ symmetry, a fact that plays a central role in dualities. In $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories, FI parameters assemble into an $SU(2)_H$ triplet, while only a single real FI parameter survives upon breaking to $\mathcal{N}=2$ .

following_context: Unlike complex masses, real masses do not require vector-like matter and are therefore available in chiral theories. In general, fields acquire real masses proportional to their charges under the corresponding global symmetry. Real mass deformations play a central role in triggering RG flows and, crucially, in generating Chern Simons and BF couplings.

following_context: It has become increasingly clear in recent years that the set of local operators in a quantum field theory is not exhausted by operators constructed as polynomials of the microscopic fields appearing in the Lagrangian . In addition to these conventional \emph{order} operators, one must also include so-called \emph{disorder} (or defect) operators, which are defined by prescribing suitable singular boundary conditions in the path integral. At first sight, local disorder operators appear to be fundamentally different from standard local operators, since their definition involves modifying the functional integration rather than inserting a local composite operator.

content: It has become increasingly clear in recent years that the set of local operators in a quantum field theory is not exhausted by operators constructed as polynomials of the microscopic fields appearing in the Lagrangian . In addition to these conventional \emph{order} operators, one must also include so-called \emph{disorder} (or defect) operators, which are defined by prescribing suitable singular boundary conditions in the path integral. At first sight, local disorder operators appear to be fundamentally different from standard local operators, since their definition involves modifying the functional integration rather than inserting a local composite operator. However, this distinction is largely an artifact of the particular choice of field variables used to describe the quantum field theory. In the full quantum theory, all local operators are on the same footing. This is reflected in the existence of numerous dualities that map order operators to disorder operators, and vice versa [Footnote: These include sine-Gordonmassive Thirring duality \cite{Coleman:1974bu, Mandelstam:1975hb}, T-duality and mirror symmetry \cite{Giveon:1994fu, Hori:2003ic} in two spacetime dimensions, and mirror symmetry and Aharony duality in three spacetime dimensions.] . In three-dimensional gauge theories, the relevant local disorder operators are \emph{monopole operators} . These operators are naturally defined in the Euclidean formulation of the theory and can be viewed as arising from dimensional reduction of ’t Hooft loop operators in four dimensions. Operationally, inserting a monopole operator $\mathfrak{M}$ into a correlation function amounts to integrating over gauge field configurations that exhibit a Dirac monopole singularity at the insertion point. More precisely, for a monopole operator of magnetic charge $m$ , one defines

In the absence of Chern Simons interactions, a \emph{bare monopole} that is, a chiral operator $\mathfrak{M}^m$ defined solely in terms of the vector multiplet can be made gauge invariant by averaging over the action of the Weyl group. More generally, the singular boundary conditions imposed on $A_\pm$ and $\sigma$ in the definition of a monopole operator break the gauge group $G$ to a residual subgroup \[ H \subset G \, , \] defined as the stabilizer of the magnetic flux $m$ , namely the subgroup satisfying $H \!\cdot m = 0$ .

content: where $\Gamma_{G^V}$ denotes the weight lattice of the Langlands dual group $G^V$ of the gauge group $G$ , and $\mathcal{W}$ is its Weyl group. In the absence of Chern Simons interactions, a \emph{bare monopole} that is, a chiral operator $\mathfrak{M}^m$ defined solely in terms of the vector multiplet can be made gauge invariant by averaging over the action of the Weyl group. More generally, the singular boundary conditions imposed on $A_\pm$ and $\sigma$ in the definition of a monopole operator break the gauge group $G$ to a residual subgroup \[ H \subset G \, , \] defined as the stabilizer of the magnetic flux $m$ , namely the subgroup satisfying $H \!\cdot m = 0$ . In the monopole background, the vector multiplets associated with $H$ remain massless, while those corresponding to the coset $G/H$ become massive. Upon integrating out these massive modes, quantum corrections are induced that modify the global and gauge charges of the monopole operator. A similar mechanism applies to matter fields. In the presence of a monopole background, matter fields that are neutral under the unbroken $U(1)\subset G \times G_F$ may acquire expectation values without breaking supersymmetry. These fields can therefore be used to \emph{dress} the bare monopole operator. Concretely, one constructs dressed monopole operators by multiplying the bare, generally gauge-variant monopole by gauge-invariant polynomials of the residual massless matter fields, yielding operators invariant under the unbroken subgroup $H$ . Finally, full $G$ -invariance is restored by averaging over the coset of Weyl groups $\mathcal{W}_G/\mathcal{W}_H$ . The presentation of monopole operators as disorder operators reflects a more modern viewpoint on $\mathcal{N}=2$ gauge theories; historically, this was not the perspective adopted in the earliest studies of mirror symmetry . In the original interpretation, monopole operators were instead motivated by a semiclassical analysis of the low-energy degrees of freedom of the theory. To illustrate this viewpoint, consider a $U(1)$ gauge theory in three dimensions. As discussed earlier, the gauge field can be dualized to a compact scalar $\gamma$ , known as the \emph{dual photon} . Together with the real scalar $\sigma$ in the $\mathcal{N}=2$ vector multiplet, one may form the complex field \[ \Phi = \sigma + i \gamma \, . \] In a semiclassical regime where the expectation value of $\Phi$ is large and the theory is weakly coupled, monopole operators admit a simple effective description. In this limit, a monopole operator carrying magnetic charge $m$ can be identified with the exponential operator [Footnote: Note that this identification can only be done for Abelian theories.] \[ \mathfrak{M}^m \;\sim\; e^{\,m \Phi} \, , \] which creates a configuration with quantized magnetic flux and carries charge under the topological $U(1)_T$ symmetry . From this perspective, monopole operators appear as ordinary local operators constructed from the effective fields of the theory, rather than as disorder operators defined by singular boundary conditions. The modern definition of monopole operators as disorder operators can be understood as a fully non-perturbative formulation of this semiclassical picture, valid throughout the quantum theory and in particular at strong coupling.

content: \medskip \textbf{Gauge charge from fermionic zero modes.} The quantum numbers of monopole operators are most efficiently computed in radial quantization, where the theory is placed on $\mathbf{S}^2\times\mathbb{R}$ with magnetic flux through $\mathbf{S}^2$ . For each charged fermion, the spectrum in a unit-flux background contains zero modes. Their contribution to the Gauss law constraint can be encoded as an effective shift of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ to the CS level. Consequently, in a $U(1)_k$ theory with $N_f$ fermions, a bare monopole with GNO flux $+1$ carries gauge charge \[ Q_{\text{gauge}} = k + \frac{N_f}{2}. \] Using $k = k_0 - \tfrac{N_f}{2}$ , this equals the bare CS level $k_0$ . Gauge-invariant operators are therefore obtained by dressing the bare monopole with appropriate powers of charged matter fields (and derivatives). \textbf{Extensions to Multiple Gauge Nodes.} The analysis generalizes straightforwardly to product gauge groups. As an illustrative example, consider a two-node abelian theory , with a bifundamental fermion of charge $(+1,-1)$ . In a monopole background, the fermionic zero modes induce: \item a $+\tfrac{1}{2}$ shift of $k_a$ , \item a $+\tfrac{1}{2}$ shift of $k_b$ , \item a $-1$ shift of the mixed CS coupling $k_{ab}$ . Let $\mathfrak{M}^{(n,m)}$ denote a bare monopole with flux $n$ under $U(1)_a$ and $m$ under $U(1)_b$ . Gauss' law implies that its gauge charges are

following_context: In the presence of a monopole background, the spin of fundamental fields in the ground state undergoes a shift — a phenomenon known as \textit{statistical transmutation} (see also ). This shift depends on the GNO charge (or flux) of the monopole, the intrinsic spin of the particle, and its gauge charge. In what follows, we summarize the effects of statistical transmutation for a particle of spin $s$ , and examine how this behavior is modified in the presence of multiple $U(1)$ gauge factors, particularly in the context of the quiver gauge theories discussed in the main text.

following_context: Consider a spin $s$ field $\varphi$ with charge $q$ under a $U(1)$ gauge group. In the background of a monopole with flux $m$ , the ground state of the field $\varphi$ has the following spin channels: For example, in the background of a monopole with flux $m=1$ a fermion has a spin- $0$ and a spin- $1$ channel, and a scalar has a spin- $\tfrac{1}{2}$ channel.

content: therefore it can be made gauge invariant with the following dressing: Each fermion involved in this dressing “sees" a GNO flux $\pm1$ , therefore it has a spin-0 zero mode. Therefore we can dress this monopole to obtain a gauge invariant spin-0 monopole operator. In the theories considered in the main paper this is one of the monopoles entering the $\mathcal{V}_{monopole}$ interaction. Similarly, we can consider the monopole operators involved in the $\mathcal{V}_{monopole}$ interaction that have GNO flux in the “bosonic" part of the quivers discussed in Section . There we find that the following monopole can be dressed to be gauge invariant and a Lorentz scalar: Notice in particular that the middle scalar involved (twice) in the dressing “sees" a GNO flux 2, while the other two scalars “see" no flux, therefore all scalars have a spin-0 mode in the background of the monopole and we obtain a spin-0 gauge invariant operator. Similarly, one can show that all the monopoles involved in the interactions $\mathcal{V}_{monopole}$ throughout Chapter can be dressed to obtain a gauge invariant operator which is a Lorentz scalar.

following_context: Another Technical Aside: The Fate of the Supersymmetric Monopole. We now review several properties of monopole operators in three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories that are repeatedly used in the main text [Footnote: It was pointed out to the author by Vittorio Cagioni that there are four possible conventions for parametrizing quantum numbers of supersymmetric monopoles, and we used all four in \cite{Benvenuti:2024seb, Benvenuti:2025a, Benvenuti:2025b,Benvenuti:2026a, Benvenuti:2026b}. Good luck.] .

following_context: As expected, the term at order $x^0$ corresponds to the vacuum. At order $x^{2/5}$ we find the gauge-invariant monopole operator, while at order $x^{4/5}$ we recover both its square and the $12$ mesonic operators $Q_i \tilde Q_j$ . following_context: In the main text, an important role is played by Abelian quiver gauge theories with mixed Chern Simons interactions and matter in bifundamental representations. We therefore summarize here the corresponding monopole charge formulas. We consider an Abelian quiver theory specified by: \item $n_g$ Abelian gauge nodes with gauge parameters $u_i$ , $i=1,\dots,n_g$ ; \item $n_f$ flavor nodes; \item A global symmetry $U(1)^r$ , excluding topological symmetries, with fugacities $y_a$ , $a=1,\dots,r$ ; \item Chern-Simons levels $k_i$ , BF couplings $k_{ij}$ , and FI parameters $\lambda_i$ , as described in Section ; \item Bifundamental chiral fields $\alpha_{ij}$ , of charge $-1$ under node $i$ and $+1$ under node $j$ ; \item Fundamental fields $\beta_{ai}$ and anti-fundamental fields $\tilde{\beta}_{ia}$ .

content: In the main text, an important role is played by Abelian quiver gauge theories with mixed Chern Simons interactions and matter in bifundamental representations. We therefore summarize here the corresponding monopole charge formulas. We consider an Abelian quiver theory specified by: \item $n_g$ Abelian gauge nodes with gauge parameters $u_i$ , $i=1,\dots,n_g$ ; \item $n_f$ flavor nodes; \item A global symmetry $U(1)^r$ , excluding topological symmetries, with fugacities $y_a$ , $a=1,\dots,r$ ; \item Chern-Simons levels $k_i$ , BF couplings $k_{ij}$ , and FI parameters $\lambda_i$ , as described in Section ; \item Bifundamental chiral fields $\alpha_{ij}$ , of charge $-1$ under node $i$ and $+1$ under node $j$ ; \item Fundamental fields $\beta_{ai}$ and anti-fundamental fields $\tilde{\beta}_{ia}$ . We consider a monopole operator with magnetic flux \( \vec{m}=(m_1,\dots,m_{n_g}) \) under the gauge nodes and vanishing flux under global symmetries. The charges of the monopole under gauge and global symmetries are compactly encoded in the polynomial

content:
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\mathcal{M}(\vec{m})
=&
-\frac{1}{2}
\Bigg[
    \sum_{\alpha_{ij}} |m_i - m_j|
    \left(
        (R[\alpha_{ij}]-1)\frac{iQ}{2}
        - u_i + u_j
        + \sum_{a=1}^{r} Q_a[\alpha_{ij}]\, y_a
    \right)
\\&\qquad
    +
    \sum_{\tilde{\beta}_{ia}} |m_i|
    \left(
        (R[\tilde{\beta}_{ia}]-1)\frac{iQ}{2}
        - u_i
        + \sum_{a=1}^{r} Q_a[\tilde{\beta}_{ia}]\, y_a
    \right)
\\&\qquad
    +
    \sum_{\beta_{ai}} |m_i|
    \left(
        (R[\beta_{ai}]-1)\frac{iQ}{2}
        + u_i
        + \sum_{a=1}^{r} Q_a[\beta_{ai}]\, y_a
    \right)
\Bigg]
\\&
    - \sum_{i=1}^{n_g} k_i m_i u_i
    + \sum_{i=1}^{n_g} \lambda_i m_i
    - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i<j} k_{ij} (m_i u_j + m_j u_i) .
\end{split}
\label{eq:monopole_charge}
\end{equation}

following_context: An important and characteristic feature of supersymmetric gauge theories, in three dimensions as well as in higher dimensions, is the existence of continuous families of degenerate vacuum solutions. These arise when the equations of motion admit configurations in which the vacuum expectation values (VEVs) of scalar fields are not uniquely fixed, but instead vary continuously without costing energy. The space parametrized by these VEVs is known as the \emph{moduli space of vacua} .

content: An important and characteristic feature of supersymmetric gauge theories, in three dimensions as well as in higher dimensions, is the existence of continuous families of degenerate vacuum solutions. These arise when the equations of motion admit configurations in which the vacuum expectation values (VEVs) of scalar fields are not uniquely fixed, but instead vary continuously without costing energy. The space parametrized by these VEVs is known as the \emph{moduli space of vacua} . Moduli spaces play a central role in both the physical and mathematical study of supersymmetric field theories. Physically, they govern the low-energy dynamics, encode patterns of spontaneous symmetry breaking, and determine the spectrum of massless degrees of freedom around a given vacuum. Mathematically, they are often realized as geometric spaces with rich structure—such as Kähler or hyperkähler manifolds—whose properties are tightly constrained by supersymmetry. The existence and structure of moduli spaces therefore provide one of the most powerful tools for analyzing supersymmetric gauge theories. In a supersymmetric gauge theory, a moduli space of vacua arises whenever the scalar potential admits continuous families of zero-energy configurations. In supersymmetric theories, the scalar potential takes the universal form

In three-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=4$ supersymmetric gauge theories, the space of supersymmetric vacua generically decomposes into distinct branches : \item \textbf{Higgs Branch:} The Higgs branch is defined as the space of vacua in which the scalar fields in the matter (hypermultiplet) sector acquire non-vanishing expectation values, while the scalar components of the vector multiplet vanish. Supersymmetry then requires the vanishing of both F-terms and D-terms, and the moduli space is parametrized by gauge-invariant composite operators, such as mesons and baryons, subject to classical constraints.

content: admit solutions that are not isolated but instead form continuous families, the space of such solutions—modulo gauge transformations—defines the moduli space of supersymmetric vacua. In three-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=4$ supersymmetric gauge theories, the space of supersymmetric vacua generically decomposes into distinct branches : \item \textbf{Higgs Branch:} The Higgs branch is defined as the space of vacua in which the scalar fields in the matter (hypermultiplet) sector acquire non-vanishing expectation values, while the scalar components of the vector multiplet vanish. Supersymmetry then requires the vanishing of both F-terms and D-terms, and the moduli space is parametrized by gauge-invariant composite operators, such as mesons and baryons, subject to classical constraints. At the classical level, the Higgs branch is described exactly by the solutions to these constraints. In $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories, the Higgs branch is fully protected against quantum corrections by the enhanced R-symmetry and remains exact at the quantum level . The Higgs branch of $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories is a hyperk \"{a} hler manifold, obtained as a hyperk \"{a} hler quotient by the gauge group . \item \textbf{Coulomb Branch:} The Coulomb branch is defined as the space of vacua in which the scalar fields in the matter multiplets have vanishing expectation values, while the scalar components of the vector multiplet acquire non-zero expectation values. As a result, the gauge symmetry is generically broken by adjoint Higgsing to a subgroup containing Abelian factors, with special loci where non-Abelian symmetry may be restored. As mentioned before, in three dimensions, each unbroken $U(1)$ factor gives rise to a dual photon $\gamma$ , a compact scalar defined by $\star F = d\gamma$ . The natural holomorphic coordinates on the Coulomb branch are provided by monopole operators, which can be viewed semiclassically as exponentials of the complex combinations $\sigma + i\gamma$ . While the Coulomb branch exists as a moduli space at the quantum level, its metric and operator dimensions generally receive quantum corrections . The Coulomb branch of $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories is also a hyperk \"{a} hler manifold due to the existence of the dual photon. As the $\mathcal{N}=4$ action contains no mixed terms involving the scalars of the matter and vector multiplets, the moduli space of $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories is a product $\mathcal{M}=\mathcal{M}_C\times \mathcal{M}_H$ .

following_context: As mentioned previously, local \emph{order} and \emph{disorder} operators in three-dimensional gauge theories are on an equal footing in the quantum theory, and any apparent distinction between them is largely an artifact of the choice of microscopic field variables used to define the Lagrangian. This observation has profound consequences for the infrared (IR) dynamics of such theories and is reflected in the existence of numerous nontrivial \emph{infrared dualities} . section_title: A First Look at Infrared Dualities
content: As mentioned previously, local \emph{order} and \emph{disorder} operators in three-dimensional gauge theories are on an equal footing in the quantum theory, and any apparent distinction between them is largely an artifact of the choice of microscopic field variables used to define the Lagrangian. This observation has profound consequences for the infrared (IR) dynamics of such theories and is reflected in the existence of numerous nontrivial \emph{infrared dualities} . Infrared dualities relate pairs of ultraviolet (UV) theories with seemingly different gauge groups, matter content, and interactions, which nevertheless flow to the same interacting fixed point at long distances. At the IR fixed point, the two descriptions become physically indistinguishable: they share the same global symmetries, spectrum of local operators, correlation functions, and moduli spaces of vacua. Crucially, duality maps gauge-invariant operators in one theory to gauge-invariant operators in the dual description, often exchanging elementary fields with composite operators and, in three dimensions, order operators with disorder (monopole) operators. Among the most well-studied examples of three-dimensional infrared dualities are the so-called \emph{Aharony-like dualities} . These dualities relate $\mathcal{N}=2$ supersymmetric gauge theories to dual theories with different gauge groups and matter content, supplemented by additional gauge-singlet fields and superpotential couplings. A defining feature of Aharony-like dualities is the exact matching of global symmetries across the dual pair, including both flavor symmetries and topological symmetries. Operators charged under these symmetries are mapped consistently between the two descriptions. In particular, monopole operators parametrizing the Coulomb branch of one theory are mapped to elementary or composite gauge-singlet fields in the dual theory, while Higgs-branch operators are mapped to Higgs-branch operators. The duality therefore provides distinct ultraviolet realizations of the same infrared fixed point, without exchanging the roles of the Higgs and Coulomb branches. We now provide a brief summary of the various IR duals of $U(N_c)_k$ SQCD. All known Aharony-like dualities can be obtained as mass deformations of the \textit{seed duality} : \item \textbf{Aharony duality (the seed duality):} The most fundamental infrared duality is

These dualities relate $\mathcal{N}=2$ supersymmetric gauge theories to dual theories with different gauge groups and matter content, supplemented by additional gauge-singlet fields and superpotential couplings. A defining feature of Aharony-like dualities is the exact matching of global symmetries across the dual pair, including both flavor symmetries and topological symmetries. Operators charged under these symmetries are mapped consistently between the two descriptions. In particular, monopole operators parametrizing the Coulomb branch of one theory are mapped to elementary or composite gauge-singlet fields in the dual theory, while Higgs-branch operators are mapped to Higgs-branch operators. The duality therefore provides distinct ultraviolet realizations of the same infrared fixed point, without exchanging the roles of the Higgs and Coulomb branches. We now provide a brief summary of the various IR duals of $U(N_c)_k$ SQCD. content: \textit{Supersymmetric localization} has emerged as one of the most powerful tools for the exact study of observables in supersymmetric quantum field theories. Its central insight is that, in the presence of sufficient supersymmetry, certain observables can be computed exactly, receiving contributions only from a restricted set of field configurations. In general, the full dynamical content of a quantum field theory is encoded in its Feynman path integral, an infinite-dimensional integral over the space of field configurations. While this object is conceptually fundamental, it is typically intractable to evaluate exactly, except in perturbative regimes. Supersymmetric localization provides a remarkable mechanism by which this infinite-dimensional integral can be reduced to a much simpler expression, often amenable to exact evaluation. The key ingredient underlying localization is supersymmetry. For supersymmetric theories—either topologically twisted or defined on curved manifolds admitting rigid supersymmetry—one can deform the action by a supersymmetry-exact term without changing the value of the partition function or of suitably protected observables. By tuning the coefficient of this deformation, the path integral localizes onto the locus of field configurations that are fixed under the chosen supercharge, known as the \emph{localization locus} . Fluctuations around this locus are captured exactly by one-loop determinants. From a mathematical perspective, supersymmetric localization can be viewed as a field-theoretic generalization of equivariant localization for finite-dimensional integrals, where integrals invariant under a symmetry group receive contributions only from the fixed points of the group action. In the quantum field theory setting, this principle leads to a dramatic reduction in complexity: a path integral over $D$ -dimensional fields may reduce to an effective path integral over $d$ -dimensional fields, with $d < D$ . In particularly favorable cases—such as when the localization locus consists of constant field configurations—this further collapses to a finite-dimensional integral, or even to a discrete sum over fixed points. Physically, localization may be understood as a situation in which the semiclassical (saddle-point or one-loop) approximation becomes exact. Importantly, this exactness does not arise from taking the original coupling $\hbar$ to zero, but instead from introducing an auxiliary deformation parameter whose vanishing limit enforces localization without altering the value of the path integral. Supersymmetric localization therefore yields non-perturbative, exact expressions for partition functions and protected observables. These results provide powerful probes of the infrared dynamics of supersymmetric gauge theories, enable precision tests of infrared dualities, and often admit elegant mathematical interpretations. In the following, we will not attempt a systematic review of localization techniques; rather, we will directly present the specific partition functions and observables that are relevant for the applications discussed in this thesis.

following_context: We now briefly review some basic properties of the partition function localized on $\mathbf{S}^2 \times \mathbf{S}^1$ , commonly referred to as the three-dimensional \emph{supersymmetric index} . When computed using the superconformal $R$ -symmetry, this partition function coincides with the \emph{superconformal index} . The index is defined as a trace over the Hilbert space of the theory quantized on $\mathbf{S}^2 \times \mathbb{R}$ ,

following_context: where $\Delta$ denotes the energy of the state (measured in units of the inverse radius of $\mathbf{S}^2$ ), $J_3$ is the Cartan generator of the $SO(3)$ isometry group of $\mathbf{S}^2$ , and $T_i$ are the charges under non- $R$ global symmetries, with corresponding fugacities $\mu_i$ . A crucial property of the supersymmetric index is that it is invariant under continuous deformations of the theory that preserve supersymmetry. This follows from the fact that only states annihilated by a chosen supercharge and its Hermitian conjugate contribute to the trace.

following_context: Brane configurations in string theory provide a powerful geometric framework for engineering and analyzing supersymmetric quantum field theories. By realizing gauge degrees of freedom as the low-energy dynamics of D-branes and matter fields as arising from open strings stretched between them, one obtains a concrete and often intuitive description of strongly coupled phenomena that are otherwise difficult to access using purely field-theoretic methods. In this approach, properties such as moduli spaces of vacua, global symmetries, and infrared dualities admit a direct geometric interpretation in terms of the relative positions and orientations of branes.

content: Brane configurations in string theory provide a powerful geometric framework for engineering and analyzing supersymmetric quantum field theories. By realizing gauge degrees of freedom as the low-energy dynamics of D-branes and matter fields as arising from open strings stretched between them, one obtains a concrete and often intuitive description of strongly coupled phenomena that are otherwise difficult to access using purely field-theoretic methods. In this approach, properties such as moduli spaces of vacua, global symmetries, and infrared dualities admit a direct geometric interpretation in terms of the relative positions and orientations of branes. In this section, we review the basic brane constructions relevant for three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories (without justifying their string origins in much detail), focusing primarily on Type IIB Hanany Witten setups. These configurations naturally realize $\mathcal{N}=4$ and $\mathcal{N}=2$ gauge theories in three dimensions and provide a unified framework for understanding mirror symmetry, real mass and Fayet Iliopoulos deformations, and a variety of infrared dualities. The results reviewed here will serve as the geometric underpinning for the mirror symmetry and duality constructions discussed in the following chapters. This section collects results from .

following_context: In addition to the fundamental string, string theory contains extended $p$ -dimensional objects known as $p$ -branes. At weak string coupling $g_s$ , these objects can be broadly classified according to how their tension scales with $g_s$ . \textit{Solitonic} , or \textit{Neveu--Schwarz} (NS), branes have tension scaling as $T \sim g_s^{-2}$ , while \textit{Dirichlet} (D) branes have tension scaling as $T \sim g_s^{-1}$ .

content: In addition to the fundamental string, string theory contains extended $p$ -dimensional objects known as $p$ -branes. At weak string coupling $g_s$ , these objects can be broadly classified according to how their tension scales with $g_s$ . \textit{Solitonic} , or \textit{Neveu--Schwarz} (NS), branes have tension scaling as $T \sim g_s^{-2}$ , while \textit{Dirichlet} (D) branes have tension scaling as $T \sim g_s^{-1}$ . This distinction is central to the non-perturbative structure of string theory and underlies many duality relations. In the low-energy supergravity description, $p$ -branes appear as extended objects electrically charged under $(p+1)$ -form gauge fields. We follow the notation of . The $(9+1)$ -dimensional spacetime of string theory is labeled by coordinates $(x^0, x^1, \ldots, x^9)$ , with associated Dirac matrices $\Gamma^M$ ( $M = 0, \ldots, 9$ ). Type IIA string theory possesses $(1,1)$ spacetime supersymmetry, with left- and right-moving supercharges satisfying

The spectrum of string theory consists of a finite number of light degrees of freedom together with an infinite tower of massive excitations, whose masses are of order the string scale or higher.

content: As a consequence, Type IIA string theory is non-chiral, whereas Type IIB string theory is chiral. The spectrum of string theory consists of a finite number of light degrees of freedom together with an infinite tower of massive excitations, whose masses are of order the string scale or higher. To make contact with low-energy physics, it is therefore convenient to focus on the dynamics of the light modes. This is achieved by integrating out the massive string excitations, yielding an effective field theory governing the low-energy degrees of freedom. Applying this procedure to Type II string theory leads to two distinct ten-dimensional low-energy effective theories: Type IIA and Type IIB supergravity. In both cases, the field content decomposes into a Neveu Schwarz Neveu Schwarz (NS NS) sector, common to the two theories, containing the spacetime metric $g_{MN}$ , the Kalb Ramond two-form $B_{MN}$ , and the dilaton $\Phi$ . The Ramond Ramond (R R) sector differs between the two theories: Type IIA supergravity contains odd-degree form potentials, while Type IIB supergravity contains even-degree form potentials. Correspondingly, the brane content of the two theories is \item \textbf{Type IIA} : $Dp$ -branes with even $p$ , namely D0, D2, D4, D6, and D8 branes. \item \textbf{Type IIB} : $Dp$ -branes with odd $p$ , namely D $(-1)$ , D1, D3, D5, and D7 branes. The D $(-1)$ brane is special, as it corresponds to a D-instanton . From a microscopic perspective, D-branes are defined as hypersurfaces on which fundamental strings can end . A $Dp$ -brane extended along the $(x^1, \ldots, x^p)$ directions and localized at a point in $(x^{p+1}, \ldots, x^9)$ is characterized by Neumann boundary conditions on the open-string endpoints along $(x^1, \ldots, x^p)$ and Dirichlet boundary conditions along $(x^{p+1}, \ldots, x^9)$ . The tension of a $Dp$ -brane is given by

The D $(-1)$ brane is special, as it corresponds to a D-instanton . From a microscopic perspective, D-branes are defined as hypersurfaces on which fundamental strings can end . A $Dp$ -brane extended along the $(x^1, \ldots, x^p)$ directions and localized at a point in $(x^{p+1}, \ldots, x^9)$ is characterized by Neumann boundary conditions on the open-string endpoints along $(x^1, \ldots, x^p)$ and Dirichlet boundary conditions along $(x^{p+1}, \ldots, x^9)$ . content: describing a $U(N)$ gauge theory with coupling $g_{\mathrm{YM}}^2 \sim g_s T_p^{-1} \alpha'^{-2}$ . The observation that a stack of $N$ coincident $Dp$ -branes gives rise to a $U(N)$ super–Yang–Mills theory with sixteen supercharges in $(p+1)$ dimensions lies at the heart of the geometric engineering of gauge theories. In this framework, gauge symmetries, matter content, and coupling constants acquire a direct geometric interpretation in terms of brane configurations and background fields. As a result, intrinsically non-perturbative phenomena in quantum field theory—such as infrared dualities, symmetry enhancement, and strong-coupling dynamics—can often be analyzed using classical or semiclassical string-theoretic tools. Geometric engineering thus provides a powerful and unifying perspective on the structure of supersymmetric gauge theories. In what follows, we will study three-dimensional gauge theories from this perspective.

content: Geometric Engineering of Three-Dimensional Results
raw_latex: \subsection{Geometric Engineering of Three-Dimensional Results}
preceding_context: As a result, intrinsically non-perturbative phenomena in quantum field theory—such as infrared dualities, symmetry enhancement, and strong-coupling dynamics—can often be analyzed using classical or semiclassical string-theoretic tools. Geometric engineering thus provides a powerful and unifying perspective on the structure of supersymmetric gauge theories. In what follows, we will study three-dimensional gauge theories from this perspective.
following_context: Picking up from the discussion of D-branes and their worldvolume dynamics, we now turn to the geometric engineering of three-dimensional gauge theories in string theory. The central idea of geometric engineering is that the low-energy dynamics of a quantum field theory can be realized as the effective description on the worldvolume of branes embedded in a suitable string-theoretic background. By appropriately choosing the type of branes, their dimensionality, and their relative orientations, one can engineer supersymmetric gauge theories with a prescribed amount of supersymmetry, matter content, and global symmetries.

content: Picking up from the discussion of D-branes and their worldvolume dynamics, we now turn to the geometric engineering of three-dimensional gauge theories in string theory. The central idea of geometric engineering is that the low-energy dynamics of a quantum field theory can be realized as the effective description on the worldvolume of branes embedded in a suitable string-theoretic background. By appropriately choosing the type of branes, their dimensionality, and their relative orientations, one can engineer supersymmetric gauge theories with a prescribed amount of supersymmetry, matter content, and global symmetries. Three-dimensional gauge theories are particularly well suited to this approach. Many of their characteristic features—such as monopole operators, topological symmetries, Chern Simons interactions, and intricate webs of infrared dualities—admit a natural and often geometric interpretation in terms of brane configurations.

content: where $SO(1,2)$ acts on the common directions $(x^0,x^1,x^2)$ , one $SO(3)$ factor acts on the directions $(x^3,x^4,x^5)$ , and the second $SO(3)$ factor acts on $(x^7,x^8,x^9)$ . The $SO(1,2)$ factor is interpreted as the Lorentz symmetry of the $(2+1)$ -dimensional worldvolume theory on the \textit{D3} -branes, while the two $SO(3)$ factors are global symmetries. In the low-energy theory, these are identified with the $SU(2)_C \times SU(2)_H$ R-symmetry of $\mathcal N=4$ supersymmetry. To analyze the low-energy physics of this configuration, it is useful to distinguish between three classes of excitations: \item bulk modes propagating in the ten-dimensional spacetime; \item modes localized on the \textit{NS5} -branes; \item modes localized on the \textit{D3} -branes. For an observer restricted to the $(2+1)$ -dimensional directions $(x^0,x^1,x^2)$ , the bulk modes and the excitations living on the \textit{NS5} -branes are effectively frozen at low energies in the weak string coupling limit. By contrast, the degrees of freedom living on the $N$ D3-branes remain dynamical, since the \textit{D3} -branes have finite extent along the $x^6$ direction. The worldvolume theory on the \textit{D3} -branes is therefore naturally viewed as a four-dimensional theory on $\mathbb R^{1,2}\times I$ , where $I$ is an interval of finite length $L_6$ along the $x^6$ direction, bounded by \textit{NS5} -branes. At energy scales much smaller than $1/L_6$ , the theory reduces effectively to a $(2+1)$ -dimensional quantum field theory. The light degrees of freedom on a stack of $N$ coincident \textit{D3} -branes consist of a four-dimensional $U(N)$ gauge field $A_\mu$ , with $\mu=0,1,2,6$ , together with six real scalar fields transforming in the adjoint representation of $U(N)$ . These scalars correspond to transverse fluctuations of the \textit{D3} -branes in the directions \[ (X^3, X^4, X^5, X^7, X^8, X^9). \] The presence of the \textit{NS5} -branes imposes boundary conditions on the fields at the ends of the interval $I$ . In particular, the fluctuations of the \textit{D3} -branes in the $(x^7,x^8,x^9)$ directions are frozen at the \textit{NS5} -brane boundaries, while the scalars $(X^3,X^4,X^5)$ remain dynamical. As a result, Kaluza Klein modes with masses of order $1/L_6$ decouple in the low-energy limit, and the remaining massless degrees of freedom organize themselves into a three-dimensional $\mathcal N=4$ \textit{vector multiplet} , consisting of the $U(N)$ gauge field $A_\mu$ ( $\mu=0,1,2$ ) and the three adjoint scalars $(X^3,X^4,X^5)$ , with gauge coupling $g_{YM}^2 \sim \tfrac{g_s}{L_6}$ . Additional matter fields arise from open strings stretching between the \textit{D3} - and \textit{D5} -branes. Each \textit{D5} -brane intersecting the \textit{D3} -branes contributes a three-dimensional $\mathcal N=4$ \textit{hypermultiplet} transforming in the fundamental representation of $U(N)$ . Thus, a configuration with $F$ \textit{D5} -branes realizes a $U(N)$ gauge theory with $F$ fundamental hypermultiplets. Geometric parameters of the brane configuration are naturally mapped to field theory couplings. Relative positions of \textit{D5} -branes in the $(x^3,x^4,x^5)$ directions correspond to real mass parameters for the hypermultiplets. Similarly, separations of \textit{NS5} -branes in the $(x^7,x^8,x^9)$ directions give rise to Fayet Iliopoulos parameters. The positions of the $N$ \textit{D3} -branes stretched between the \textit{NS5} -branes in the transverse directions $(x^3,x^4,x^5)$ , together with the $N$ dual photons arising from the unbroken $U(1)^N$ gauge symmetry, parametrize the $4N$ -dimensional Coulomb branch of the three-dimensional $\mathcal N=4$ gauge theory. These moduli correspond to vacuum expectation values of the scalars in the vector multiplet and their dual photons. From the perspective of the fivebrane worldvolume theory, however, this moduli space admits a different and highly instructive interpretation. The theory living on the pair of \textit{NS5} -branes is a six-dimensional $\mathcal N=(1,1)$ supersymmetric gauge theory with gauge group $U(2)$ , which is spontaneously broken to $U(1)\times U(1)$ by the separation of the fivebranes. In this description, \textit{D3} -branes stretched between the \textit{NS5} -branes are identified with magnetic monopole strings of the broken $SU(2)$ gauge theory . Consequently, the $4N$ -dimensional Coulomb branch of the three-dimensional theory is naturally identified, from the fivebrane viewpoint, with the moduli space of $N$ BPS monopoles.

content: Type IIB brane configuration engineering three-dimensional $\mathcal N=2$ supersymmetric gauge theories. The presence of both \textit{NS5} and rotated \textit{NS5} $'$ fivebranes breaks supersymmetry to four supercharges, schematically shown in Figure . The presence of both \textit{NS5} and \textit{NS5} $'$ fivebranes breaks the rotational symmetry in the transverse directions and reduces the preserved supersymmetry from $\mathcal N=4$ to $\mathcal N=2$ . The relative orientation of the fivebranes lifts the adjoint chiral multiplet present in $\mathcal N=4$ theories, leaving only a single real scalar in the vector multiplet, as expected for $\mathcal N=2$ supersymmetry in three dimensions.

content: Aharony Duality from Geometric Engineering. Aharony duality admits a natural and geometrically transparent realization in the Hanany Witten brane construction of three-dimensional $\mathcal N=2$ supersymmetric gauge theories. We demonstrate this here by recovering Aharony duality from the $\mathcal N=2$ setup of Table . A key ingredient in the brane realization of the duality is the \textit{Hanany--Witten effect} . When the \textit{NS5} $'$ -brane is adiabatically moved past a \textit{D5} -brane along the $x^6$ direction, a \textit{D3} -brane is created stretching between the \textit{NS5} $'$ -brane and the \textit{D5} -brane in order to preserve charge conservation and supersymmetry. As the \textit{NS5} $'$ -brane crosses all $N_f$ \textit{D5} -branes, a total of $N_f$ \textit{D3} -branes are created. Taking into account the original $N_c$ color \textit{D3} -branes, the resulting configuration contains $N_f - N_c$ \textit{D3} -branes suspended between the \textit{NS5} and \textit{NS5} $'$ branes, realizing a dual $U(N_f - N_c)$ gauge theory at low energies. In addition to the color branes, the brane crossing produces semi-infinite \textit{D3-} branes stretching between the \textit{NS5} $'$ -brane and the \textit{D5} -branes. From the three-dimensional perspective, these degrees of freedom are non-dynamical gauge singlets. Their presence is essential: they are naturally identified with the chiral multiplets that are dual to the electric monopole operators. Geometrically, monopole operators correspond to \textit{D1} -strings stretched between the \textit{NS5} and \textit{NS5} $'$ branes, and the couplings between these \textit{D1} -strings and the semi-infinite \textit{D3} -branes induce superpotential interactions that flip the monopoles of the dual theory. An important consistency condition in this construction is the \emph{s-rule} (also see ), which states that at most a single supersymmetric \textit{D3} -brane can stretch between an \textit{NS5} -brane and a \textit{D5} -brane while preserving supersymmetry. In the present context, the s-rule ensures that the number of color \textit{D3} -branes suspended between the \textit{NS5} and \textit{NS5} $'$ branes is bounded by $N_f$ , and in particular that a supersymmetric vacuum exists only when $N_f \geq N_c$ . This constraint has a direct field-theoretic interpretation: it mirrors the condition for the existence of a stable supersymmetric vacuum in three-dimensional $\mathcal N=2$ SQCD and guarantees that the brane configuration correctly encodes the infrared dynamics of the theory. Thus, we see that embedding QFTs in string theory sheds light on a lot of properties which are not \textit{a priori} visible. Furthermore, the geometric properties of string theory are such that it helps relate QFTs in ways far from obvious. The Hanany Witten construction not only provides a geometric realization of Aharony duality, but also furnishes a unifying framework in which a wide class of three-dimensional dualities arise from simple operations on brane configurations. Among these, \emph{mirror symmetry} occupies a distinguished position, admitting a particularly transparent geometric interpretation in Type IIB string theory. Mirror symmetry is the central theme of this thesis, and we will review its basic structure and physical implications in greater detail in the following section.

preceding_context: The Hanany Witten construction not only provides a geometric realization of Aharony duality, but also furnishes a unifying framework in which a wide class of three-dimensional dualities arise from simple operations on brane configurations. Among these, \emph{mirror symmetry} occupies a distinguished position, admitting a particularly transparent geometric interpretation in Type IIB string theory. Mirror symmetry is the central theme of this thesis, and we will review its basic structure and physical implications in greater detail in the following section.

content: Quiver diagrams ( ) are central to our work, and we take a brief pause to outline our conventions regarding the same here. The low-energy effective field theory associated with an HW setup is often conveniently encoded in terms of \textit{quiver diagrams} . A quiver diagram is an oriented diagram built with the following ingredients (following ): \item \textbf{Nodes} (or vertices) encode the symmetries of the theory. Gauge symmetries are represented by circular nodes, while global symmetries are denoted by squares. In what follows, we consider classical Lie groups and summarize their respective contributions to the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function below: \item the $\mathcal{N}=2$ vector multiplet associated with a \textbf{unitary} [Footnote: The contribution of the $\mathcal{N}=2$ vector multiplet associated with a \textbf{special unitary} symmetry can be obtained by a straightforward modification of the integration measure of the unitary symmetry as follows: \int\prod_\alpha=1^Ndu_\alpha\to \int\prod_\alpha=1^Ndu_\alpha\,\delta\bigg(\sum_\alpha=1^Nu_\alpha\bigg). An $SU(N)$ gauge symmetry will be reported as a double circled node labeled as $N$.] symmetry is reported as follows: where $\{u_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha=1}^N$ are the fugacities associated with the generators of the Cartan subalgebra of the $U(N)$ gauge symmetry, the Chern-Simons level is indicated in red below the node, and the fugacity of the topological $U(1)_T\in U(N)$ (denoted by $\eta$ ) is indicated in orange above the node. \item the $\mathcal{N}=2$ vector multiplet associated with a \textbf{symplectic} symmetry is reported as follows: where $\{u_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha=1}^N$ are the fugacities associated with the generators of the Cartan subalgebra of the $USp(2N)$ gauge symmetry, and the Chern-Simons level is indicated in red below the node. \item the $\mathcal{N}=2$ vector multiplet associated with a \textbf{special orthogonal} symmetry is reported as follows: where $\{u_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha=1}^N$ are the fugacities associated with the generators of the Cartan subalgebra of the $SO(2N+1)$ and $SO(2N)$ gauge symmetries, and the Chern-Simons level is indicated in red below the node. \item \textbf{Edges} represent matter fields of the theory. \item The bifundamental $\mathcal{Q}$ with R-charge $R$ transforming under various symmetry groups contributes to the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function as follows: Note that an edge directed into (outside) a node denotes an anti-fundamental (fundamental) representation. \item The presence of an adjoint superfield in the theory is indicated by a closed loop: Their contributions to the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function can be inferred from those of the respective vector multiplets. \item The presence of a field with R-charge $R$ in the rank-2 antisymmetric representation of a unitary symmetry (and its conjugate) is indicated as follows: where $\{X_i\}_{i=1}^N$ are the fugacities associated with the generators of the Cartan subalgebra of the $U(N)$ symmetry, and $s$ is the mass of the antisymmetric field. \item The presence of a mixed CS interaction at level $k_{XY}$ between the $U(1)\subset U(N)_{\vec{X}}$ and $U(1)\subset U(M)_{\vec{Y}}$ is reported as follows: \item There is a superpotential interaction associated with each \textbf{cycle} of the quiver diagram. For instance, consider the following quiver diagram: In what follows, such interactions will be collectively denoted as $\mathcal{W}_{planar}$ . Quiver diagrams are central to this work, as they provide a compact encoding of all matter content and interactions.

following_context: Unlike ordinary perturbative symmetries, which are visible order by order in a weak-coupling expansion, a \emph{nonperturbative quantum symmetry} is not manifest at the perturbative level. In general, establishing such a symmetry would require complete control over the full quantum theory, including its strongly coupled regime. What makes duality symmetries particularly powerful is that, in supersymmetric theories, they can often be inferred using only weak-coupling and semiclassical data, without detailed knowledge of the full nonperturbative dynamics.

content: Unlike ordinary perturbative symmetries, which are visible order by order in a weak-coupling expansion, a \emph{nonperturbative quantum symmetry} is not manifest at the perturbative level. In general, establishing such a symmetry would require complete control over the full quantum theory, including its strongly coupled regime. What makes duality symmetries particularly powerful is that, in supersymmetric theories, they can often be inferred using only weak-coupling and semiclassical data, without detailed knowledge of the full nonperturbative dynamics. Supersymmetry imposes strong constraints on the structure of quantum corrections. In theories with sufficient supersymmetry, many semiclassical quantities receive no quantum corrections neither perturbative nor nonperturbative and are therefore exact. Such protected quantities can be computed reliably in the weak-coupling regime and then analytically continued to strong coupling. If two theories are related by a duality, all nonrenormalized observables must agree. The requirement that these quantities match provides highly nontrivial consistency checks, which are often compelling enough to establish the existence of a duality even in the absence of a rigorous proof . Type IIB string theory provides a canonical example of this phenomenon. In addition to discrete $\mathbb{Z}_2$ perturbative symmetries, it enjoys a nonperturbative $SL(2;\mathbb{Z})$ duality symmetry, which is visible at the level of the equations of motion governing the axio dilaton field. The modular group $SL(2;\mathbb{Z})$ is generated by the elements

following_context: Provided that strong-coupling effects do not break supersymmetry, consistency implies that Type IIB string theory can only be dual to itself under this symmetry. There are two classes of semiclassical quantities that are expected to be exact and must therefore exhibit the $SL(2;\mathbb{Z})$ duality group: \item the massless spectrum and the corresponding equations of motion; \item the spectrum of BPS-saturated states. Consequently, one expects the nonperturbative $SL(2;\mathbb{Z})$ invariance of Type IIB string theory to manifest itself as a duality symmetry in Type IIB Hanany Witten brane configurations.

The $\mathcal S$ transformation therefore exchanges the sectors of the theory associated with vector multiplets and hypermultiplets. At the level of the moduli space of vacua, this corresponds to an exact exchange of the Coulomb and Higgs branches (both of which are hyperk \"{a} hler manifolds ) at the infrared fixed point.

content: More precisely, in the Hanany Witten construction, \textit{NS5} -branes engineer vector multiplet degrees of freedom on the \textit{D3} -branes, while \textit{D5} -branes engineer hypermultiplet matter. The $\mathcal S$ transformation therefore exchanges the sectors of the theory associated with vector multiplets and hypermultiplets. At the level of the moduli space of vacua, this corresponds to an exact exchange of the Coulomb and Higgs branches (both of which are hyperk \"{a} hler manifolds ) at the infrared fixed point. The central statement of $\mathcal N=4$ \textbf{mirror symmetry} is thus that it maps perturbative Higgs-branch operators, such as mesons and baryons, to intrinsically nonperturbative Coulomb-branch operators, namely monopole operators. From this perspective, mirror symmetry can be understood as an infrared duality \textit{that exchanges flavor symmetries with topological symmetries} of the three-dimensional theory. It is important to stress that this mechanism is qualitatively different from Aharony-like dualities in three dimensions. While Aharony duality relates distinct ultraviolet gauge theories that flow to the same infrared fixed point, it does not exchange topological and flavor symmetries. In contrast, $\mathcal N=4$ mirror symmetry precisely interchanges these symmetries, mapping flavor currents on one side to topological currents on the other. Mirror symmetry is therefore a distinct type of infrared duality, characterized by an exact exchange of Higgs and Coulomb branch data rather than a rearrangement of matter content within a fixed global symmetry structure.

following_context: We now make these ideas concrete by considering three-dimensional $\mathcal N=4$ SQCD with gauge group $U(N)$ and $F$ hypermultiplets. This theory admits a particularly simple realization in Type IIB string theory via the Hanany Witten brane configuration summarized in Table . The action of Type IIB $\mathcal S$ -duality on this setup naturally leads to mirror pairs of $\mathcal N=4$ theories, as shown in Figure .

section_title: \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal N=4$ Unitary Mirror Symmetry}{N=4 Unitary Mirrors}
content:
Action of $\mathcal S$-duality on a Type IIB HananyWitten configuration.
The left panel shows the original setup realizing $\mathcal N=4$ $U(N)$ SQCD with
$F$ hypermultiplets: $N$ \textit{D3}-branes are suspended between two \textit{NS5}-branes,
with $F$ \textit{D5}-branes providing the flavors of the theory.
Applying the $\mathcal S$ element of $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ exchanges \textit{NS5}- and
\textit{D5}-branes, yielding the configuration shown in the middle panel.
This intermediate configuration violates the \textit{s-rule} and therefore does
not preserve supersymmetry.
To restore supersymmetry, one must perform a sequence of HananyWitten moves,
during which \textit{D3}-branes are created or annihilated as fivebranes cross.
The resulting supersymmetric configuration, shown in the right panel,
realizes the mirror dual theory.

content:
Mirror duality for $\mathcal N=4$ SQCD with gauge group $U(N)$ and
    $F$ flavors, depicted in $\mathcal N=2$ notation. Circular nodes denote
    gauge groups and square nodes denote flavor symmetry groups, while arrows
    represent chiral multiplets charged under the corresponding nodes. Black
    labels indicate fugacities associated with flavor symmetries, while orange
    labels denote FI parameters for the gauge groups.

content: where $U(1)_C \subset SU(2)_C$ and $U(1)_H \subset SU(2)_H$ . From the $\mathcal N=2$ perspective, $U(1)_R$ plays the role of the R-symmetry, while $U(1)_\tau$ should be regarded as a global symmetry. We do not fix the exact superconformal R-charges. Instead, we assign trial charges such that the fundamental and anti-fundamental chiral multiplets have R-charge $1$ and $U(1)_\tau$ charge $-\tfrac{1}{2}$ , while adjoint chiral multiplets have R-charge $0$ and $U(1)_\tau$ charge $1$ . On the electric side, we denote by $\Phi$ , $Q$ , and $\tilde Q$ the adjoint and fundamental/anti-fundamental chiral multiplets of SQCD. On the mirror side, we use the notation $\Phi_J$ , $b_{J,J+1}$ , and $\widetilde b_{J+1,J}$ for the adjoint and bifundamental chiral multiplets of the linear quiver, together with fundamental chiral multiplets $q,\tilde q$ and $p,\tilde p$ at the two ends of the quiver.

[Footnote: Although two $U(1)$ flavor nodes appear in the quiver, the $\mathcal N=4$ superpotential ensures that only one independent combination, denoted by $\eta$, is realized faithfully.] A highly nontrivial prediction of mirror symmetry is that the topological symmetries of the mirror theory enhance in the infrared to an $SU(F)$ flavor symmetry, matching exactly the global symmetry of the SQCD theory. This enhancement has been verified using a variety of complementary methods: matching the infrared metrics on Higgs and Coulomb branches [Footnote: This result was stated in a more mathematically precise language by \cite{Intriligator_1996}, and we repeat it here: the Higgs branch of Kronheimer \cite{Kronheimer:1989zs} gauge theories is an \textit{almost-locally Euclidean} (ALE) space, while the Coulomb branch gives the moduli space of a small ADE instanton.

following_context: Mapping of gauge-invariant operators. Mirror symmetry also provides a precise correspondence between gauge-invariant operators of the two theories . In particular, \item The fundamental monopole operators $\mathfrak{M}^{\pm}$ of the SQCD theory are mapped to long mesonic operators on the mirror quiver side.

content: Mapping of gauge-invariant operators. Mirror symmetry also provides a precise correspondence between gauge-invariant operators of the two theories . In particular, \item The fundamental monopole operators $\mathfrak{M}^{\pm}$ of the SQCD theory are mapped to long mesonic operators on the mirror quiver side. \item The electric mesons, transforming in the traceless adjoint representation of $SU(F)$ , are mapped to a collection of monopole operators in the mirror theory. These assemble into an adjoint-valued matrix of $SU(F)$ constructed from the traces of the adjoint fields $\Phi_J$ together with monopole operators carrying GNO flux under consecutive gauge nodes: monopoles with flux $\pm1$ under a single node, monopoles with flux $\pm1$ under two adjacent nodes, and so on, up to monopoles carrying flux $\pm1$ under all $F-1$ gauge nodes. Mirror symmetry has a natural interpretation for theories with $\mathcal N=4$ supersymmetry, and is seen as a consequence of $\mathcal S$ -duality inherited from IIB string theory. As such, it is an infrared duality that relates theories with swapped flavor and topological symmetries.

following_context: What makes theories with \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal N<4$}{N<4} so intractable? The subject of this thesis is the study of mirror symmetry in three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories with $\mathcal N<4$ supersymmetry. This regime is substantially more subtle than the well-understood $\mathcal N=4$ case.

content: What makes theories with \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal N<4$}{N<4} so intractable? The subject of this thesis is the study of mirror symmetry in three-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories with $\mathcal N<4$ supersymmetry. This regime is substantially more subtle than the well-understood $\mathcal N=4$ case. As we had mentioned before, $\mathcal N=4$ mirror symmetry may be intuitively seen as an automorphism on the supersymmetry algebra that exchanges the two $SU(2)$ factors of the R-symmetry. When the amount of supersymmetry is reduced, this intuition fails, as the R-symmetry does not have identical factors (e.g.: the R-symmetry of $\mathcal N=3$ theories is $SO(3)\cong SU(2)$ , while that of $\mathcal N=2$ theories is $SO(2) \cong U(1)$ ) that can be exchanged. More precisely, when the amount of supersymmetry is reduced, the Higgs and Coulomb branches may not factorize into distinct product manifolds, or may be partially or completely lifted, and the canonical Type IIB brane realizations that underlie mirror symmetry are often no longer available [Footnote: It was shown in \cite{Benvenuti:2023qtv} that applying $\mathcal{S}-$duality on $\mathcal N=2$ brane setups (like the one shown in Figure \ref{n=2hw}) is possible, but the resulting dual description is inherently non-Lagrangian since it is built with superconformal matter.] . As a consequence, mirror duals of $\mathcal N< 4$ theories are not known in general and cannot be systematically derived from brane constructions. Their identification therefore requires intrinsically field-theoretic tools. The aim of this thesis is to develop such methods and to propose and test mirror-like dualities in broad classes of three-dimensional theories with reduced supersymmetry. Throughout this work, we are guided by the defining feature of mirror symmetry: \textbf{it is a duality that exchanges topological and flavor symmetries, mapping order and disorder operators to each other.} Thus, even when geometric engineering techniques no longer exist, constraints from arguments based on symmetries are strong enough for us to generalize this notion to theories with minimal supersymmetry. \newpage
