section_title: An Electric Fermion With A Positive Mass
content: Upon integrating out the massive fermion, the electric theory flows to $SU(3)$ QCD $_3$ with $N_s$ scalars and $N_f-1$ fermions at CS level $6-N_s-\frac{N_f}{2}+\frac{1}{2}$ . On the mirror side, the deformation cause the horizontal scalars to take a negative mass and thus a VEV, Higgsing pairs of $U(1)$ gauge groups connected by a horizontal scalar to the diagonal $U(1)$ . This effectively identifies the two columns of $U(1)$ gauge groups to the left and right of the column of scalars. Furthermore, some of the cubic interactions for the scalars turn into effective masses, which allow one to integrate out the $2$ diagonal scalars and $2$ of the vertical scalars. The resulting quiver is shown in the bottom row of Figure . The resulting duality coincides with our proposed dual for $SU(N)$ with $N_s$ scalars and $N_f-1$ fermions at CS level $2N-N_s-\frac{(N_f-1)}{2}$ , where $N=3$ in this case. \newpage \newpage Immediate generalizations to electric gauge groups with higher rank follow from this observation. We reiterate that integrating out an electric fermion with a positive mass results in Higgsing the horizontal bosons in a column in the bulk of its planar mirror, thereby shortening the quiver. One can check that CS and BF coupling of the resulting theory agree with our proposal (Figure ), providing a consistency check of our results.

We reiterate that integrating out an electric fermion with a negative mass results in the integration of a column of bosons in the bulk of its planar mirror, leaving behind only BF couplings between horizontal gauge nodes. We conclude by mentioning that in the duality , each fermion in the QCD $_3$ corresponds to a column of bosons in the dual theory. Therefore, the results summarized in Figure and can be used to study the effect on the dual theory of a mass for any number of fermions in the QCD $_3$ .

following_context: Instead of studying mass deformations of individual bosonic or fermionic fields, one may consider turning on masses for all matter fields simultaneously. In particular, we can give positive masses to all scalars and a homogeneous mass to all fermions in the QCD $_3$ theory. Such deformations generically gap out the matter sector and drive the theory to a purely topological infrared phase, described by a TQFT.

section_title: Duals of Topological Quantum Field Theories
content: Instead of studying mass deformations of individual bosonic or fermionic fields, one may consider turning on masses for all matter fields simultaneously. In particular, we can give positive masses to all scalars and a homogeneous mass to all fermions in the QCD $_3$ theory. Such deformations generically gap out the matter sector and drive the theory to a purely topological infrared phase, described by a TQFT. Since the original non-supersymmetric duality relates two theories that are expected to flow to the same IR fixed point, the corresponding mass-deformed theories should also flow to dual TQFTs. However, a direct analysis of these flows in the non-supersymmetric setting rapidly becomes complicated. A more tractable strategy is therefore to exploit the existence of supersymmetric ancestor dualities: one can study gapped phases of the corresponding $\mathcal{N}=2$ theories, where the renormalization group flows are better controlled, and then infer the resulting TQFT dualities. We expect that the TQFTs obtained in this way coincide with those arising directly from mass-deforming the non-supersymmetric dual pairs. A detailed analysis of these supersymmetric flows is presented in . Here, we focus on the outcome in two representative cases, corresponding to SQCD theories whose Abelian planar duals contain either one or zero bulk columns.

following_context: We first consider the duality for $SU(N)_{-1}$ QCD with $N_s = 2N$ scalars and no fermions, shown in Figure . Assigning positive masses to all scalars gaps the matter fields and leaves behind a pure $SU(N)_{-1}$ Chern-Simons theory in the infrared. On the Abelian side, the planar quiver dual contains a single empty bulk column (see Figure ).

content: We first consider the duality for $SU(N)_{-1}$ QCD with $N_s = 2N$ scalars and no fermions, shown in Figure . Assigning positive masses to all scalars gaps the matter fields and leaves behind a pure $SU(N)_{-1}$ Chern-Simons theory in the infrared. On the Abelian side, the planar quiver dual contains a single empty bulk column (see Figure ). This structure plays a crucial role in determining the resulting topological theory. More generally, the analysis described here applies to any QCD theory whose Abelian dual contains exactly one bulk column. Rather than following the non-supersymmetric flow directly, we consider a chiral-planar $\mathcal{N}=2$ duality studied in , in which the electric theory flows to the same $SU(N)_{-1}$ TQFT after appropriate mass deformations. In the supersymmetric setting, these deformations can be explicitly tracked on the planar Abelian side , leading to an Abelian Chern-Simons theory with gauge group $U(1)^2$ and Chern Simons matrix

In the remainder of this section, we provide an independent check of this claim by deriving the duality using known TQFT equivalences.

The corresponding supersymmetric analysis, together with its planar Abelian dual, predicts that the dual theory flows to a $U(1)_1$ Chern Simons theory.

content: A similar analysis applies to $SU(N)_0$ QCD with $N_s = 2N$ scalars. Giving positive masses to all scalars drives the theory to pure $SU(N)_0$ Yang Mills in the infrared. The corresponding supersymmetric analysis, together with its planar Abelian dual, predicts that the dual theory flows to a $U(1)_1$ Chern Simons theory. This is an almost trivial TQFT, in agreement with the expected infrared behavior of the electric theory.

following_context: In this section, we attempt to study theories where the number of scalars $N_s$ is less than $2N$ . We find that we are able to consistently \textit{bosonize} two columns of height $N-1$ outside of the bulk of the quiver gauge theory, which means that in the duality we replace the tallest column in the two triangular sections with a column made only by bosons. Each replacement of columns using this strategy consists of decreasing/increasing the number of bosons/fermions by 1 in duality .

content: In this section, we attempt to study theories where the number of scalars $N_s$ is less than $2N$ . We find that we are able to consistently \textit{bosonize} two columns of height $N-1$ outside of the bulk of the quiver gauge theory, which means that in the duality we replace the tallest column in the two triangular sections with a column made only by bosons. Each replacement of columns using this strategy consists of decreasing/increasing the number of bosons/fermions by 1 in duality . We emphasize that this can be done to reduce the number of bosons by 2 at most. This strategy allows us to propose a quiver which is dual to a QCD $_3$ theory with $N_s=2N-1$ or $N_s = 2N-2$ scalars and $N_f$ fermions such that $N_s + N_f \geq 2N$ , generalizing the dualities discussed in Section . Extending this procedure to even lower $N_s$ by considering quivers with more bosonic columns in the outer triangle would require a generalization of our prescription to construct monopole interactions. We leave this to future work. We illustrate this proposal using two examples, one with $N_s = 2N-1$ and one with $N_s = 2N-2$ . In particular, we notice that the $SU(2)$ QCD $_3$ when $N_s=2$ and $N_f \geq 2$ is dual to a completely bosonic planar theory.

In particular, we notice that the $SU(2)$ QCD $_3$ when $N_s=2$ and $N_f \geq 2$ is dual to a completely bosonic planar theory.

content: Notice that some of the monopole operators in Table seem to factorize, in the sense that they can be thought of as the product of two independent gauge invariant monopoles. We recall, however, that there are additional monopoles with the same global charges and spin, which will, in general, mix. The monopoles reported in Table are representatives of such linear combinations. \\ In line with our observation in the last section, we can consistently bosonize" the entire quiver. This is a special feature of $SU(2)$ , and we anticipate that the planar dual of $SU(2)_{2-\frac{N_f}{2}-k}$ ( $k\geq 0$ ) with $2$ scalars and $N_f$ fermions will be a fully bosonic Abelian quiver gauge theory.

following_context: We restrict ourselves to the study of $SU(N)$ QCD $_3$ with at least $N_s=2N-2$ bosonic fundamental flavors and any number of fermionic fundamental flavors. Extending our proposal to study duals for smaller values of $N_s$ would be the next step. This can be achieved by further mass deformations of the present dualities, as discussed in for the $\mathcal{N}=2$ supersymmetric case.

content: We restrict ourselves to the study of $SU(N)$ QCD $_3$ with at least $N_s=2N-2$ bosonic fundamental flavors and any number of fermionic fundamental flavors. Extending our proposal to study duals for smaller values of $N_s$ would be the next step. This can be achieved by further mass deformations of the present dualities, as discussed in for the $\mathcal{N}=2$ supersymmetric case. Another promising direction of research would be to develop an algorithmic paradigm to construct these bosonization dualities, in a vein similar to their $\mathcal{N}=2$ and $\mathcal{N}=4$ counterparts. Such an approach would help construct planar Abelian duals for broader classes of non-Abelian gauge theories. \newpage
raw_latex: We restrict ourselves to the study of $SU(N)$ QCD $_3$ with at least $N_s=2N-2$ bosonic fundamental flavors and any number of fermionic fundamental flavors. Extending our proposal to study duals for smaller values of $N_s$ would be the next step. This can be achieved by further mass deformations of the present dualities, as discussed in for the $\mathcal{N}=2$ supersymmetric case. Another promising direction of research would be to develop an algorithmic paradigm to construct these bosonization dualities, in a vein similar to their $\mathcal{N}=2$ and $\mathcal{N}=4$ counterparts. Such an approach would help construct planar Abelian duals for broader classes of non-Abelian gauge theories. \newpage

content: Conclusions and Outlook
raw_latex: \chapter{Conclusions and Outlook}
following_context: Mirror symmetry for $\mathcal N=4$ theories admits a compelling and intuitive interpretation in terms of Type IIB string theory and Hanany Witten brane constructions. In this setting, the exchange of Higgs and Coulomb branches, as well as the corresponding interchange of flavor and topological symmetries, is geometrically manifest. However, this intuition ceases to apply once the amount of supersymmetry is reduced.

content: Mirror symmetry for $\mathcal N=4$ theories admits a compelling and intuitive interpretation in terms of Type IIB string theory and Hanany Witten brane constructions. In this setting, the exchange of Higgs and Coulomb branches, as well as the corresponding interchange of flavor and topological symmetries, is geometrically manifest. However, this intuition ceases to apply once the amount of supersymmetry is reduced. For theories with $\mathcal N<4$ , the Higgs and Coulomb branches may be lifted or collapse to isolated vacua, and the canonical brane realizations underlying mirror symmetry are often absent. As a result, \textit{geometric engineering alone is no longer a reliable guide} . The primary goal of this thesis has been to investigate whether mirror-like dualities can nevertheless persist in theories with $\mathcal N=2$ , and even in the absence of supersymmetry. Despite the breakdown of string-theoretic intuition, symmetry considerations remain sufficiently powerful to uncover and test such dualities. In particular, we find that the defining structural feature of mirror symmetry — namely, the \emph{exchange of topological and flavor symmetries} — continues to provide a robust organizing principle beyond the $\mathcal N=4$ setting. From this perspective, mirror symmetry should be viewed more generally as a duality that maps \emph{perturbative, order-type operators to non-perturbative, disorder-type operators} , such as monopole operators, rather than as a consequence of a specific brane construction. This viewpoint allows mirror symmetry to be formulated and tested intrinsically within quantum field theory, opening the door to systematic explorations of dualities in theories with reduced, or no, supersymmetry. A central result of this thesis is the development of the paradigm of \textbf{Planar Abelianization} , which proves especially compelling for several reasons: \item The resulting $\mathcal N=2$ mirror duals are Abelian \emph{and} admit a Lagrangian description — two highly nontrivial and \textit{a priori} independent properties. \item The construction admits straightforward generalizations to $A_N$ and $D_N$ quivers, theories with symplectic and special unitary gauge groups, circular quivers, and models with rich matter content, including adjoint fields, rank-2 antisymmetric tensors, as well as the presence of both fundamental and anti-fundamental representations . \item The framework enables systematic deformations, yielding theories with more general Chern Simons levels and flavor content . For special choices of parameters, the resulting theories display a rich infrared phenomenology, such as $s$ -confinement, chiral symmetry breaking, and the emergence of non-Abelian TQFTs. \item The paradigm further admits extensions beyond supersymmetry, encompassing non-supersymmetric dualities . In addition to the future directions discussed at the end of each chapter, we now briefly outline several further avenues of investigation suggested by the results of this thesis. [Footnote: These are broadly based on projects started and subsequently abandoned by the author.]

These constructions typically give rise to quivers with alternating orthogonal and symplectic gauge groups. section_title: The Road to Orthosymplectic Mirror Symmetry
content: Mirror duals of $\mathcal N=4$ orthogonal and symplectic SQCD can be engineered using \emph{Feng-Hanany} brane setups , which incorporate orientifold planes in addition to the standard Hanany–Witten configurations. These constructions typically give rise to quivers with alternating orthogonal and symplectic gauge groups. Even in the presence of $\mathcal{N}=4$ supersymmetry, analyzing these theories remains highly nontrivial. This complexity arises from the emergence of various symmetries acting on the moduli space in the deep infrared, which are absent in the UV Lagrangian and hence invisible to supersymmetric localization. A number of tools, such as the \textit{magnetic quivers} paradigm, have been developed to study these theories , but a complete and detailed analysis remains intricate. Both $\mathcal{N}=4$ $U(N)$ and $USp(2N)$ SQCD with $F$ hypermultiplets admit RG flows under supersymmetry-breaking deformations to $\mathcal{N}=2$ vacua in which the gauge symmetries are Higgsed to their maximal tori, $U(1)^N$ . Finding these vacua is nontrivial, but they form the building blocks of algorithmic planar dual constructions, which proceed by gluing together \textit{planarized} " $U(N)$ or $USp(2N)$ blocks. A natural question is whether $SO(2N)$ and $SO(2N+1)$ SQCD with $F$ hypermultiplets also admit such flows. The answer is affirmative: these theories likewise reach $\mathcal{N}=2$ vacua in which the gauge symmetries are Higgsed to $U(1)^N$ , as illustrated in Equation . This observation is important because, even if a fully planar Abelianization of orthosymplectic quivers is not yet known, the existence of these planar limits suggests that a proto-algorithm for constructing orthosymplectic planar duals may still be feasible. \newpage

following_context: Mirror symmetry also admits a rich realization in two dimensions. In contrast to its three-dimensional avatar , \emph{two-dimensional mirror symmetry} does not involve monopole operators or topological symmetries. Instead, it relates seemingly different descriptions of $\mathcal N=(2,2)$ supersymmetric quantum field theories.

content: Mirror symmetry also admits a rich realization in two dimensions. In contrast to its three-dimensional avatar , \emph{two-dimensional mirror symmetry} does not involve monopole operators or topological symmetries. Instead, it relates seemingly different descriptions of $\mathcal N=(2,2)$ supersymmetric quantum field theories. In its simplest form, it maps a non-linear sigma model with a K \"a hler target space to a Landau Ginzburg theory with a superpotential, exchanging geometric data with purely holomorphic information . From a physical perspective, this duality exchanges momentum and winding modes and interchanges complex-structure and K \"a hler moduli, providing a powerful non-perturbative equivalence between theories with very different ultraviolet descriptions. It is natural to expect a deeper connection between three-dimensional and two-dimensional mirror symmetry . However, the reduction of three-dimensional dualities to two-dimensional ones is a delicate matter , for several reasons. \item Supersymmetric theories with four supercharges often possess a classical moduli space of vacua (at least for suitable values of their parameters), and two-dimensional theories are no exception. This moduli space can be modified or lifted by quantum corrections. In higher dimensions, each point on the quantum moduli space corresponds to a distinct vacuum lying in a different superselection sector, and the low-energy theory at a generic point is free, while interacting dynamics arise only near singular loci. In contrast, in two dimensions quantum fluctuations are strong enough that the ground state (and, more generally, all states) explore the entire target space or moduli space. \item The sigma-model metric is classically marginal in two dimensions and is generically renormalized. As a result, it cannot be neglected in the analysis of infrared dynamics. This issue is particularly acute for non-compact target spaces or in the presence of runaway directions, where supersymmetric configurations may lie at infinite distance. In such cases, there may be no normalizable ground state, and instead a continuum of states can extend down to zero energy, depending on the asymptotic behavior of the K \"a hler potential. Such continua can contribute to supersymmetric indices in ways that differ qualitatively from the contributions of isolated vacua. \item A phenomenon unique to two dimensions is the possibility that a single ultraviolet supersymmetric theory can flow to more than one superconformal field theory in the infrared. More precisely, the infrared limit may be a direct sum of decoupled SCFTs, so that the Hilbert space is a sum rather than a tensor product of the individual Hilbert spaces. These features become particularly significant when compactifying three-dimensional $\mathcal N=2$ theories on a circle. In this context, different ways of taking the radius of the circle to zero can lead to distinct two-dimensional conformal field theories lying on the same conformal manifold. Constructing mirror duals of two-dimensional $\mathcal N=(2,2)$ theories thus remains a formidable challenge. A variety of complementary approaches have been developed, including analyses based on A- and B-twists, constraints from K-theory , and the study of topologically twisted indices . The approach we advocate builds on the results of . Upon supersymmetric localization of three-dimensional theories on $\mathbf S^2 \times \mathbf S^1$ or $\mathbf D^2 \times \mathbf S^1$ , the resulting partition functions admit distinct asymptotic limits as the radius of $\mathbf S^1$ is taken to zero. The precise infrared limit depends on how real mass parameters and Fayet Iliopoulos terms are scaled in this process. Concretely, two inequivalent limits can be defined:

following_context: In the \emph{Higgs limit} , matter fields remain light and the effective theory localizes onto the Higgs branch. The resulting partition function reduces to that of a two-dimensional theory on $\mathbf S^2$ or on $\mathbf D^2$ , depending on the geometry. By contrast, in the \emph{Coulomb limit} all matter fields become heavy and are integrated out.

content: In the \emph{Higgs limit} , matter fields remain light and the effective theory localizes onto the Higgs branch. The resulting partition function reduces to that of a two-dimensional theory on $\mathbf S^2$ or on $\mathbf D^2$ , depending on the geometry. By contrast, in the \emph{Coulomb limit} all matter fields become heavy and are integrated out. The resulting expression can be identified with a Dotsenko Fateev conformal block , and, \textit{with some significant mathematical gymnastics} , can be recast into the twisted superpotential of a Landau-Ginzburg model with a logarithmic twisted superpotential. For a pair of three-dimensional mirror dual theories, the two limits described above are not independent. Mirror symmetry exchanges the Higgs and Coulomb branches, and therefore taking the Coulomb limit of one theory necessarily forces the Higgs limit of its mirror dual, and vice versa. As a result, mirror symmetry in three dimensions descends to a nontrivial equivalence between distinct two-dimensional limits of the same parent theory. It is therefore natural to apply this framework to the chiral–planar mirror dualities studied in this work. By taking the Coulomb limit on one side of the duality and the Higgs limit on the other, one obtains pairs of $\mathcal N=(2,2)$ partition functions which are expected to coincide. Studying these limits provides a concrete and quantitative probe of mirror symmetry in two dimensions and offers a promising route toward identifying and testing mirror duals of non-Abelian $\mathcal N=(2,2)$ theories. [Footnote: These problems are interesting mathematical questions in their own right and may provide insight into toric fibrations over Grassmannian manifolds and into the interplay between branes, mirror symmetry, and toric geometry \cite{Vianna_2014,evans2022lectures,Leung:1997tw,buchstaber2019, Witten:1993xi, Gu_2021}.]

following_context: Brane configurations for five-dimensional $\mathcal N=1$ gauge theories were first constructed in , following the general framework pioneered in , using Type IIB \textit{D5} - and \textit{NS5} -branes. In Type IIB string theory, fivebranes transform as a $(p,q)$ multiplet under the $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ duality group, where the \textit{D5} -brane corresponds to a $(0,1)$ fivebrane and the \textit{NS5} -brane to a $(1,0)$ fivebrane. At the classical level, a configuration in which a \textit{D5} -brane ends on an \textit{NS5} -brane preserves eight supercharges.

content: Brane configurations for five-dimensional $\mathcal N=1$ gauge theories were first constructed in , following the general framework pioneered in , using Type IIB \textit{D5} - and \textit{NS5} -branes. In Type IIB string theory, fivebranes transform as a $(p,q)$ multiplet under the $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ duality group, where the \textit{D5} -brane corresponds to a $(0,1)$ fivebrane and the \textit{NS5} -brane to a $(1,0)$ fivebrane. At the classical level, a configuration in which a \textit{D5} -brane ends on an \textit{NS5} -brane preserves eight supercharges. Quantum mechanically, however, this picture is modified: the \textit{NS5} -brane bends due to a Coulomb-like interaction sourced by the attached \textit{D5} -brane . A more accurate description is that charge conservation forbids a pure \textit{D5} -brane from ending on a pure \textit{NS5} -brane. Instead, at the junction the two combine into a bound state carrying both charges, known as a \textit{$(1,1)$ fivebrane} . More generally, bound states of $(p,q)$ fivebranes can be formed whenever the $(p,q)$ charges are conserved. The resulting webs of $(p,q)$ fivebranes provide a powerful geometric framework for engineering five-dimensional $\mathcal N=1$ gauge theories. Analyzing the interacting five-dimensional worldvolume theories supported on these configurations is an interesting and formidable problem, which we will not mention beyond this. $(p,q)$ fivebranes also play an important role in the geometric engineering of three-dimensional gauge theories, where they naturally give rise to Chern Simons interactions. From this perspective, it is natural to expect that the \textit{chiral--planar mirror duals} studied in this thesis may admit a Type IIB realization in terms of brane tilings constructed from $(p,q)$ fivebranes. Such a description would provide a geometric underpinning for these dualities and merits a renewed and systematic investigation of their properties. studied the low-energy effective theory on the worldvolume of two \textit{D3-} branes ending a $(1,K)$ -web on each side. Such $\mathcal N=2$ theories display a non-trivial \textit{tetrality} , shown in Equation , where we have suppressed the details of the superpotential for brevity. \newpage By invoking the $FM[U(N)]$ \textit{generalized bifundamental block} [Footnote: The $FM[U(N)$ theory is a (famously self-mirror) 3d $\mathcal N=2$ SCFT which displays an $SU(N)\times SU(N)\times U(1)^2$ global symmetry at its infrared fixed point. The gauge invariant spectrum of this theory includes two adjoint operators and two bifundamental operators charged under the two $SU(N)$ global symmetries. Theories built with \textit{superconformal matter} as building blocks are inherently non-Lagrangian, and these theories are no exception.] , it is possible to generalize this tetrality to $\mathcal N=2$ non-Abelian gauge theories, shown in Equation . We have once again suppressed the details of the superpotential for brevity. It would be interesting to evaluate the connection of such constructions to our \textit{chiral-planar mirror dual pairs} , and to study the low-energy effective theory of a more general $(p,q)$ -web suspended between $N$ \textit{D3} -branes.

following_context: We remarked earlier that Type IIB string theory enjoys an $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ duality symmetry, and that mirror symmetry for $\mathcal N=4$ theories admits a natural interpretation in terms of the action of the $\mathcal S$ generator. For $\mathcal N=4$ gauge theories, the $\mathcal S$ transformation is implemented by coupling to the $FT[U(N)]$ theory. The $\mathcal T$ generator of $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ , on the other hand, corresponds to a shift of the Chern Simons level of a background gauge field .

content: We remarked earlier that Type IIB string theory enjoys an $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ duality symmetry, and that mirror symmetry for $\mathcal N=4$ theories admits a natural interpretation in terms of the action of the $\mathcal S$ generator. For $\mathcal N=4$ gauge theories, the $\mathcal S$ transformation is implemented by coupling to the $FT[U(N)]$ theory. The $\mathcal T$ generator of $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ , on the other hand, corresponds to a shift of the Chern Simons level of a background gauge field . As in the case of $\mathcal S$ , the action of $\mathcal T$ admits a local interpretation on brane configurations. Building on this observation, a systematic $\mathcal N=4$ dualization algorithm was introduced in , in which general duality transformations are constructed as sequences of $\mathcal S$ and $\mathcal T$ operations acting locally on brane configurations. This framework reveals a large web of three-dimensional dualities unified by the action of a single $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ structure. We have already encountered the action of the $\mathcal S$ generator in the context of $\mathcal N=2$ theories in this thesis. It is therefore natural to ask whether the paradigm of planar Abelianization can be extended to construct $\mathcal N=2$ analogues of the $\mathcal T$ generator. Pursuing this direction suggests the existence of an $\mathcal N=2$ realization of the $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ dualization algorithm. As in the $\mathcal N=4$ case, such a framework is expected to organize and generate large classes of dualities among three-dimensional theories with reduced supersymmetry and connect our $\mathcal N=2$ \textit{chiral-planar mirror duals} to other existing constructions .

content: Higher Dimensional Constructions
raw_latex: \chapter{Higher Dimensional Constructions}
following_context: Many of the structures and dualities encountered in lower-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories admit a unifying interpretation when viewed from a higher-dimensional perspective. In particular, a large class of four- and three-dimensional supersymmetric quantum field theories can be obtained by compactifying the six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ superconformal field theory on suitably chosen manifolds. This viewpoint underlies the construction of \emph{Class $\mathcal{S}$} theories, as well as the AGT correspondence relating supersymmetric gauge theories to two-dimensional conformal field theories.

content: Many of the structures and dualities encountered in lower-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories admit a unifying interpretation when viewed from a higher-dimensional perspective. In particular, a large class of four- and three-dimensional supersymmetric quantum field theories can be obtained by compactifying the six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ superconformal field theory on suitably chosen manifolds. This viewpoint underlies the construction of \emph{Class $\mathcal{S}$} theories, as well as the AGT correspondence relating supersymmetric gauge theories to two-dimensional conformal field theories. The purpose of this appendix is to provide a brief and self-contained review of these ideas, with an emphasis on their conceptual role rather than on technical details. We summarize the essential features of the six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ theory, its compactification on Riemann surfaces leading to Class $\mathcal{S}$ constructions, and the basic statement of the AGT correspondence. This discussion is intended solely to place the results of the main text in a broader theoretical context, and we will not attempt an exhaustive or pedagogical treatment of these subjects.

content: Six-dimensional superconformal field theories play a distinguished role in the landscape of supersymmetric quantum field theories, often serving as \emph{master theories} from which many lower-dimensional constructions descend. This privileged status stems from the fact that superconformal algebras do not exist in spacetime dimensions $D>6$ . The obstruction arises from the additional constraints imposed by superconformality on Lie superalgebras: the bosonic subalgebra $\mathfrak{g}_0$ must act on the fermionic generators $\mathfrak{g}_1$ in a spinorial representation . Remarkably, this requirement is satisfied only marginally in six dimensions. The triality properties of the complexified conformal group $SO(6,2;\mathbb{C})$ allow vector representations of $\mathfrak{g}_0$ to be interchanged with spinorial representations of $\mathfrak{g}_1$ , thereby permitting the existence of superconformal symmetry. No such construction is possible in higher dimensions, rendering six dimensions the upper critical dimension for interacting SCFTs. The on-shell representations of six-dimensional supersymmetry are well known and consist of the following multiplets : \item the \textbf{hypermultiplet} , containing four real scalars and two spin- $\tfrac{1}{2}$ fermions; \item the \textbf{vector multiplet} , containing a vector field and two gaugini; \item the \textbf{tensor multiplet} , containing a real scalar, a spin- $\tfrac{1}{2}$ fermion, and a two-form gauge field with anti-self-dual field strength; \item the \textbf{gravity multiplet} , containing the graviton, two gravitini, and a chiral two-form gauge field with self-dual field strength. The requirement that the theory admit a conserved stress-energy tensor places strong restrictions on the allowed amount of supersymmetry. In six dimensions, this implies that interacting superconformal theories can have at most sixteen supercharges, corresponding to $\mathcal{N}=(1,0)$ , $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ , or $\mathcal{N}=(1,1)$ supersymmetry. Six-dimensional SCFTs are intrinsically non-gravitational theories. As such, they are built from combinations of hypermultiplets, vector multiplets, and tensor multiplets, but do not include dynamical gravity. A crucial and somewhat counterintuitive feature of these theories is that, unlike in lower dimensions, interacting conformal field theories in six dimensions do not generally admit a Lorentz-covariant Lagrangian description. Indeed, only trivial examples—such as the free scalar theory

following_context: —possess a conventional local action. In particular, $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ superconformal theories necessarily contain tensor multiplets, whose anti-self-dual two-form fields obstruct the existence of a manifestly Lorentz-covariant action. As a result, these theories are best understood through indirect means, such as their operator content, symmetry structure, and protected observables, rather than through a microscopic Lagrangian formulation.

content: —possess a conventional local action. In particular, $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ superconformal theories necessarily contain tensor multiplets, whose anti-self-dual two-form fields obstruct the existence of a manifestly Lorentz-covariant action. As a result, these theories are best understood through indirect means, such as their operator content, symmetry structure, and protected observables, rather than through a microscopic Lagrangian formulation. This conceptual impasse was resolved during the Second String Revolution, which revealed a geometric and string-theoretic origin for six-dimensional SCFTs . The key insight is that such theories can arise as decoupling limits of string or M-theory compactifications: by compactifying extra dimensions, decoupling gravity, and tuning parameters to special loci in moduli space, one encounters regimes in which effective strings become tensionless. The resulting interacting fixed points furnish precisely the six-dimensional superconformal field theories that lack a conventional Lagrangian description. A remarkable property of six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ superconformal field theories is that interacting theories are classified by simply-laced Lie algebras of ADE type. That is, there exists a family of $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ SCFTs labeled by $\mathfrak{g} = A_{N-1}, D_N, E_6, E_7,$ and $E_8$ , with no known interacting theories outside this classification. This ADE classification admits a natural geometric interpretation in string and M-theory. $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ theories of type $\mathfrak{g}$ arise as the low-energy worldvolume theories of coincident M5-branes, or equivalently from Type IIB string theory compactified on ALE spaces with ADE singularities. In this picture, the appearance of ADE algebras is tied to the classification of isolated $\mathbb{C}^2/\Gamma_{\mathrm{ADE}}$ singularities, and the tensionless strings of the six-dimensional theory originate from M2-branes wrapping vanishing two-cycles at the singular locus. Although six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ SCFTs do not admit a conventional Lagrangian description, their physical content becomes accessible upon compactification. Compactifying these theories on a circle produces maximally supersymmetric five-dimensional Yang Mills theories, while compactification on lower-dimensional manifolds gives rise to a rich web of supersymmetric quantum field theories in four and three dimensions. In particular, many dualities and non-perturbative phenomena observed in lower-dimensional supersymmetric gauge theories can be understood as descending from geometric operations—such as degenerations or dualities of the compactification manifold—at the level of the six-dimensional parent theory. For this reason, six-dimensional SCFTs provide a unifying framework for a wide class of supersymmetric field theories and dualities.

following_context: As discussed above, interacting six-dimensional superconformal field theories do not admit a conventional Lagrangian description. Nevertheless, a powerful handle on their dynamics is obtained by compactifying them on lower-dimensional manifolds. In particular, one may consider compactifying a six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ SCFT on a Riemann surface $\mathcal{C}$ , performing a partial topological twist to preserve supersymmetry.

content: As discussed above, interacting six-dimensional superconformal field theories do not admit a conventional Lagrangian description. Nevertheless, a powerful handle on their dynamics is obtained by compactifying them on lower-dimensional manifolds. In particular, one may consider compactifying a six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ SCFT on a Riemann surface $\mathcal{C}$ , performing a partial topological twist to preserve supersymmetry. The resulting lower-dimensional theories inherit much of their structure from the geometry of $\mathcal{C}$ and from the choice of defects inserted along it. The four-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=2$ theories obtained in this way are known as \emph{Class $\mathcal{S}$} theories [Footnote: The notation $\mathcal{S}$, much to the author's disappointment, stands for six.] . These constructions provide a geometric framework for understanding a vast class of supersymmetric quantum field theories, encoding their matter content, couplings, and dualities in terms of the topology and complex structure of the underlying Riemann surface .

From a purely four-dimensional perspective, the appearance of the Seiberg Witten curve and its relation to the low-energy prepotential may seem somewhat ad hoc.

content: Seiberg-Witten Geometry. As a motivating example, we focus on \textit{Seiberg--Witten geometry} in four-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=2$ gauge theories . From a purely four-dimensional perspective, the appearance of the Seiberg Witten curve and its relation to the low-energy prepotential may seem somewhat ad hoc. This structure, however, becomes much more natural when viewed through the lens of Class $\mathcal{S}$ constructions. In this framework, the Seiberg Witten curve is not an auxiliary object introduced to solve the theory, but rather has a direct geometric origin: it is precisely the Riemann surface on which the parent six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ theory is compactified. The Seiberg Witten geometry of the four-dimensional theory is therefore inherited directly from the compactification geometry of the underlying six-dimensional SCFT.

content: The AGT Correspondence. Another important duality uncovered using these constructions is the \textit{4d/2d-correspondence} , or the \textit{Alday-Gaiotto-Tachikawa} (AGT) correspondence . The basic idea can be summarized as follows. One starts from a six-dimensional SCFT $\chi(\mathfrak g)$ and considers its partition function $\mathcal{Z}$ on a product manifold of the form $\mathcal{W}_4 \times \mathcal{C}_2$ , where $\mathcal{W}_4$ is a four-dimensional manifold and $\mathcal{C}_2$ is a two-dimensional Riemann surface. The resulting quantity $\mathcal{Z}[\mathcal{W}_4 \times \mathcal{C}_2]$ depends in a controlled way on the geometric and topological properties of both factors. Crucially, the same partition function can be evaluated in two equivalent ways, depending on which factor is regarded as the compactification manifold:

following_context: That is, one may either interpret the result as a two-dimensional theory living on $\mathcal{C}_2$ , obtained by compactifying the six-dimensional theory on $\mathcal{W}_4$ , or as a four-dimensional theory on $\mathcal{W}_4$ , obtained by compactification on $\mathcal{C}_2$ . In terms of observables, the AGT correspondence relates $4d$ partition functions to $2d$ conformal blocks.

following_context: The 3d/3d Correspondence. A closely related example of this philosophy is the \textit{3d/3d correspondence} , which relates supersymmetric quantum field theories in three dimensions to geometric and topological data of three-manifolds . Starting from the six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ theory of type $\mathfrak g$ , one considers its compactification on a three-manifold $M_3$ , possibly with a partial topological twist.

content: The 3d/3d Correspondence. A closely related example of this philosophy is the \textit{3d/3d correspondence} , which relates supersymmetric quantum field theories in three dimensions to geometric and topological data of three-manifolds . Starting from the six-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=(2,0)$ theory of type $\mathfrak g$ , one considers its compactification on a three-manifold $M_3$ , possibly with a partial topological twist. This construction gives rise to a three-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=2$ theory, often denoted $T[M_3]$ , whose protected observables capture invariants of $M_3$ . As in the AGT correspondence, the same six-dimensional partition function may be evaluated by exchanging the roles of spacetime and internal geometry. In particular, supersymmetric partition functions of $T[M_3]$ on curved three-manifolds—such as $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ or $\mathbf{S}^2\times\mathbf{S}^1$ —are related to topological invariants of $M_3$ , including Chern Simons theory and quantum invariants of knots and three-manifolds. The $3d/3d$ correspondence thus provides a powerful bridge between three-dimensional supersymmetric field theories, low-dimensional topology, and quantum geometry. It would be interesting to study the chiral-planar dualities presented in this thesis with the machinery of Class $\mathcal S$ . While it seems possible to recover $2d$ $\mathcal N=(2,2)$ mirror symmetry by compactifying the chiral-planar mirror pairs on $\mathbf{S}^1$ , it may also be possible to study the holomorphic blocks of the resulting theory to arrive at the putative partition function of a $4d$ $\mathcal N=1$ theory ( \textit{a la} ). In this way, we may realise a rich web of dualities across dimensions.

In particular, supersymmetric partition functions of $T[M_3]$ on curved three-manifolds—such as $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ or $\mathbf{S}^2\times\mathbf{S}^1$ —are related to topological invariants of $M_3$ , including Chern Simons theory and quantum invariants of knots and three-manifolds. The $3d/3d$ correspondence thus provides a powerful bridge between three-dimensional supersymmetric field theories, low-dimensional topology, and quantum geometry. It would be interesting to study the chiral-planar dualities presented in this thesis with the machinery of Class $\mathcal S$ . While it seems possible to recover $2d$ $\mathcal N=(2,2)$ mirror symmetry by compactifying the chiral-planar mirror pairs on $\mathbf{S}^1$ , it may also be possible to study the holomorphic blocks of the resulting theory to arrive at the putative partition function of a $4d$ $\mathcal N=1$ theory ( \textit{a la} ). In this way, we may realise a rich web of dualities across dimensions.

following_context: By composing the duality operation shown in Equation , one can derive mirror dual pairs of SQED with a generic number of hypermultiplets \textit{a la} Kapustin-Strassler . We provide a brief summary of the piecewise dualization procedure needed to find the mirror of $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQED with $F$ flavors . Each flavor is dualized using Equation to obtain a $U(1)^{F+1}$ with $2F$ charged fields, $F$ \textit{flippers} (and a corresponding superpotential) and $2F$ mixed BF interactions connecting the original $U(1)$ gauge group with the $2F$ new gauge factors.

content: By composing the duality operation shown in Equation , one can derive mirror dual pairs of SQED with a generic number of hypermultiplets \textit{a la} Kapustin-Strassler . We provide a brief summary of the piecewise dualization procedure needed to find the mirror of $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQED with $F$ flavors . Each flavor is dualized using Equation to obtain a $U(1)^{F+1}$ with $2F$ charged fields, $F$ \textit{flippers} (and a corresponding superpotential) and $2F$ mixed BF interactions connecting the original $U(1)$ gauge group with the $2F$ new gauge factors. At this point, the original $U(1)$ gauge group has no flavors and we can perform the functional integral over it obtaining a functional delta function. Implementing the delta reduces the number of gauge groups to $F-1$ , which is indeed the correct mirror dual. To dualize the planar quivers in this paper, we need some additional self-dualities of $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQED with $F=2$ . The starting point of this is the $\mathcal{N}=2$ self-dual ( \textit{modulo} flips) pair shown in Equation : with the operator map:

section_title: An Application: Recovering the Symmetric Bifundamental Block from a Planar Abelian Quiver Gauge Theory
content: In Equation , it was demonstrated that $N$ free chiral fields are mirror dual to a planar quiver gauge theory. This is schematically repeated in Figure . Our aim in this section is to sequentially confine the planar quivers using only the pairs of Aharony duals discussed before. We demonstrate how to reduce the planar mirror of an $[N,N]$ bifundamental block to the planar mirror of an $[N-1, N-1]$ bifundamental block by following a sequence of columnwise Aharony dualizations here. Performing this sequence iteratively will confine the planar mirror.
raw_latex: In Equation , it was demonstrated that $N$ free chiral fields are mirror dual to a planar quiver gauge theory. This is schematically repeated in Figure . Our aim in this section is to sequentially confine the planar quivers using only the pairs of Aharony duals discussed before. We demonstrate how to reduce the planar mirror of an $[N,N]$ bifundamental block to the planar mirror of an $[N-1, N-1]$ bifundamental block by following a sequence of columnwise Aharony dualizations here. Performing this sequence iteratively will confine the planar mirror.

Performing this sequence iteratively will confine the planar mirror.

content: For clarity, we fix \( N = 3 \) . We begin with the planar mirror of the \([3,3]\) bifundamental [Footnote: We follow the same conventions for the Chern–Simons, mixed Chern–Simons, and FI interactions as before, and will only mention them explicitly when they differ from these standard assignments. The FI term associated with the gauge node of fugacity $u_{3,3}$ receives an additional contribution of $-\tfrac{iQ}{4}$ due to mixing with the $U(1)_R$ symmetry which has been suppressed for brevity.] : We now perform a sequence of local Aharony dualizations, column by column. As this is our first example, we are pedantic and provide many details. We first provide a schematic summary of the same in Figure before diving into the details. \item \textbf{Column 1} : \item \textbf{Column 2} [Footnote: As before, we suppress the mixing between the topological and $U(1)_R$ symmetries in this quiver. The gauge nodes with fugacities $u_{3,3}$ and $u_{2,2}$ each receive an additional contribution of $-\tfrac{iQ}{4}$, while the node $u_{2,1}$ receives a contribution of $+\tfrac{iQ}{4}$.] : \item \textbf{Column 3} [Footnote: The gauge nodes with fugacities $u_{2,2}$ and $u_{4,2}$ receive an additional contribution of $-\tfrac{iQ}{4}$, while the gauge node with fugacity $u_{3,1}$ receives an additional contribution of $+\tfrac{iQ}{4}$ to their respective FI terms.] : Notice that we make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{3,3}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_4-X_1}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 4} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{4,2}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_5-X_1}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 5} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{5,1}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_6-X_1}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 4} : \item \textbf{Column 3} : \item \textbf{Column 2} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{2,2}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_6-X_3}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 1} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{1,1}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_6-X_2}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. We recognize the resulting quiver as the planar mirror of the \([2,2]\) bifundamental block. \item \textbf{Column 2} : \item \textbf{Column 3} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{3,2}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_4-X_2}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 4} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{4,1}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_5-X_2}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item \textbf{Column 2} : We make use of the duality shown in Equation here. Consequently, the gauge node with fugacity $u_{2,1}$ confines to generate a singlet. The topological symmetry of the gauge node is $U(1)_{X_4-X_3}$ ; consequently, the singlet generated upon dualizing and confining this node will carry a $+1$ charge under this symmetry. \item We make use of the duality shown in Equation once again. The gauge node with fugacity $u_{3,1}$ confines and generates a singlet with charge $+1$ under $U(1)_{X_5-X_3}$ . Thus, we correctly recover all 9 components of the $[3,3]$ bifundamental block. Thus, we observe that the planar mirror of an \([N,N]\) bifundamental block can be systematically confined to yield the planar mirror of an \([N{-}1, N{-}1]\) bifundamental block. This is achieved by sequentially dualizing each column of the planar quiver—first from left to right, and then again from right to left.
